Given: Saving = 15000 each
Therefore, we have,
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} {15000} \\ {15000} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} {3 x} \\ {4 x} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{c} {5 y} \\ {7 y} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
So,
3 x – 5 y = 15000 …(1)

Solving equations (1)and (2),we get,
Multiplying eq(1) by 4 and eq(2) by 3 we get,
12 x – 20 y = 60000 ….(3)
12 x – 21 y = 45000 …..(4)
Eq(3) – Eq(4),
Y = 15000
Putting this value in eq(1) we get,
3 x – 5{tex}\times{/tex} 15000 = 15000

3x-75000=15000
3 x = 90000
X = 30000
There monthly incomes are, 3 x = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 30000= 90000 and
4 x = 4 {tex}\times{/tex}  30000= 120000

question_html: A trust invested some money in two type of bonds. The first bond pays 10% interest and second bond pays 12% interest. The trust received ₹ 2800 as interest. However, if trust had interchanged money in bonds, they would have got ₹ 100 less as interest. Using matrix method, find the amount invested by the trust.
question_text: A trust invested some money in two type of bonds. The first bond pays 10% interest and second bond pays 12% interest. The trust received ₹ 2800 as interest. However, if trust had interchanged money in bonds, they would have got ₹ 100 less as interest. Using matrix method, find the amount invested by the trust.
explanation: Given that some amount is invested into two types of bonds which gives as 10% and 12% interest rates respectively. Let the amount invested in bonds of the first type and the second type be Rs x and Rs y respectively. Hence, the amount invested in each type of the bonds can be represented in matrix form with each column corresponding to a different type of bond as - X = [X Y] The annual interest obtained is Rs 2800. We know the formula to calculate the simple interest on a principal of Rs P at a rate R% per annum for t years is given by, Interest = {tex}\frac{\text { PTR }}{100}{/tex} Here, the time is one year and thus T = 1. Hence, the interest obtained after one year can be expressed in matrix representation as - [x y] {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} \frac{10}{100} \\ \frac{12}{100} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = [2800] {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\mathrm{x} \times \frac{10}{100}+\mathrm{y} \times \frac{12}{100}\right]=[2800]{/tex} since the both sides of the equaation are matrices of order 1x1,we can equate the corresponding elements.We get {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{10 x}{100}+\frac{12 y}{100}{/tex} = 2800 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10x + 12y = 2800 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10x + 12y = 280000 {tex}\therefore{/tex} 5x + 6y = 140000 …… (1) However, on reversing the invested amounts, the interest received is Rs 100 less than the earlier value (Rs 2800). Now, the amount invested in the second bond is Rs x and that in the first bond is Rs y with the annual interest obtained being Rs 2700. Hence, the interest obtained by exchanging the invested amount of the two bonds after one year can be expressed in matrix representation as – [x y] {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} \frac{10}{100} \\ \frac{12}{100} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = [2700] {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\mathrm{y} \times \frac{10}{100}+\mathrm{x} \times \frac{12}{100}\right]=[2700]{/tex} since the both sides of the equaation are matrices of order 1x1,we can equate the corresponding elements.We get {tex}\Rightarrow \frac{10 \mathrm{y}}{100}+\frac{12 \mathrm{x}}{100}{/tex} = 2700 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10y + 12x = 2700 {tex}\times{/tex} 100 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 12x + 10y = 270000 ∴ 6x + 5y = 135000 …… (2) Recall that the solution to the system of equations that can be written in the form AX = B is given by X = A –1 B. Here, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 5 & 6 \\ 6 & 5 \end{array}\right], X=\left[\begin{array}{l} X \\ y \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 140000 \\ 135000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} We know the inverse of a matrix A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} a & b \\ c & d \end{array}\right]{/tex} is given by A -1 = {tex}\frac{1}{|A|}{/tex} adj (A) = {tex}\frac{1}{a d-b c}\left[\begin{array}{cc} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{array}\right]{/tex} |A| = (5)(5) – (6)(6) = 25 – 36 = –11 ( for the given matrix A) {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{A}^{-1}=\frac{1}{-11}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 & -6 \\ -6 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} We have X = A –1 B. {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{X}=\frac{1}{-11}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 & -6 \\ -6 & 5 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} 140000 \\ 135000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l} ^{X} \\ y \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{-11}\left[\begin{array}{l} (5)(140000)+(-6)(135000) \\ (-6)(140000)+(5)(135000) \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{x} \\ \mathrm{y} \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{-11}\left[\begin{array}{l} 700000-810000 \\ -840000+675000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{x} \\ \mathrm{y} \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{-11}\left[\begin{array}{l} -110000 \\ -165000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{x} \\ \mathrm{y} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} \frac{-110000}{-11} \\ \frac{-165000}{-11} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{x} \\ \mathrm{y} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 10000 \\ 15000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Amount invested in the first bond = x = Rs 10000 Amount invested in the second bond = y = Rs 15000 Thus, the trust invested Rs 10000 in the first bond and Rs 15000 in the second bond.

Here, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 5 & 6 \\ 6 & 5 \end{array}\right], X=\left[\begin{array}{l} X \\ y \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 140000 \\ 135000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} We know the inverse of a matrix A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} a & b \\ c & d \end{array}\right]{/tex} is given by A -1 = {tex}\frac{1}{|A|}{/tex} adj (A) = {tex}\frac{1}{a d-b c}\left[\begin{array}{cc} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{array}\right]{/tex} |A| = (5)(5) – (6)(6) = 25 – 36 = –11 ( for the given matrix A) {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{A}^{-1}=\frac{1}{-11}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 & -6 \\ -6 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} We have X = A –1 B. {tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{X}=\frac{1}{-11}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 & -6 \\ -6 & 5 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} 140000 \\ 135000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l} ^{X} \\ y \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{-11}\left[\begin{array}{l} (5)(140000)+(-6)(135000) \\ (-6)(140000)+(5)(135000) \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{x} \\ \mathrm{y} \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{-11}\left[\begin{array}{l} 700000-810000 \\ -840000+675000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{x} \\ \mathrm{y} \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{-11}\left[\begin{array}{l} -110000 \\ -165000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{x} \\ \mathrm{y} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} \frac{-110000}{-11} \\ \frac{-165000}{-11} \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{x} \\ \mathrm{y} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 10000 \\ 15000 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Amount invested in the first bond = x = Rs 10000 Amount invested in the second bond = y = Rs 15000 Thus, the trust invested Rs 10000 in the first bond and Rs 15000 in the second bond.
solution_html:
Given that some amount is invested into two types of bonds which gives as 10% and 12% interest rates respectively.
Let the amount invested in bonds of the first type and the second type be Rs x and Rs y respectively.
Hence, the amount invested in each type of the bonds can be represented in matrix form with each column corresponding to a different type of bond as -
X = [X Y]
The annual interest obtained is Rs 2800.
We know the formula to calculate the simple interest on a principal of Rs P at a rate R% per annum for t years is given by,
Interest = {tex}\frac{\text { PTR }}{100}{/tex}
Here, the time is one year and thus T = 1.
Hence, the interest obtained after one year can be expressed in matrix representation as -
[x y]{tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} \frac{10}{100} \\ \frac{12}{100} \end{array}\right]{/tex}= [2800]
{tex}\Rightarrow\left[\mathrm{x} \times \frac{10}{100}+\mathrm{y} \times \frac{12}{100}\right]=[2800]{/tex} since the both sides of the equaation are matrices of order 1x1,we can equate the corresponding elements.We get 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{10 x}{100}+\frac{12 y}{100}{/tex} = 2800
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10x + 12y = 2800 {tex}\times{/tex} 100
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10x + 12y = 280000
{tex}\therefore{/tex} 5x + 6y = 140000 …… (1)
However, on reversing the invested amounts, the interest received is Rs 100 less than the earlier value (Rs 2800).
Now, the amount invested in the second bond is Rs x and that in the first bond is Rs y with the annual interest obtained being Rs 2700.
Hence, the interest obtained by exchanging the invested amount of the two bonds after one year can be expressed in matrix representation as –
[x y]{tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} \frac{10}{100} \\ \frac{12}{100} \end{array}\right]{/tex}= [2700]
{tex}\Rightarrow\left[\mathrm{y} \times \frac{10}{100}+\mathrm{x} \times \frac{12}{100}\right]=[2700]{/tex} since the both sides of the equaation are matrices of order 1x1,we can equate the corresponding elements.We get 
{tex}\Rightarrow \frac{10 \mathrm{y}}{100}+\frac{12 \mathrm{x}}{100}{/tex} = 2700
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 10y + 12x = 2700 {tex}\times{/tex} 100
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 12x + 10y = 270000
∴ 6x + 5y = 135000 …… (2)
Recall that the solution to the system of equations that can be written in the form AX = B is given by X = A–1B.
Here, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 5 & 6 \\ 6 & 5 \end{array}\right], X=\left[\begin{array}{l} X \\ y \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 140000 \\ 135000 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
We know the inverse of a matrix A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} a & b \\ c & d \end{array}\right]{/tex} is given by
A-1 = {tex}\frac{1}{|A|}{/tex} adj (A) = {tex}\frac{1}{a d-b c}\left[\begin{array}{cc} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{array}\right]{/tex}

|A| = (5)(5) – (6)(6) = 25 – 36 = –11 ( for the given matrix A)
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{A}^{-1}=\frac{1}{-11}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 & -6 \\ -6 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
We have X = A–1B.
{tex}\Rightarrow \mathrm{X}=\frac{1}{-11}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 & -6 \\ -6 & 5 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} 140000 \\ 135000 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l} ^{X} \\ y \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{-11}\left[\begin{array}{l} (5)(140000)+(-6)(135000) \\ (-6)(140000)+(5)(135000) \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{x} \\ \mathrm{y} \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{-11}\left[\begin{array}{l} 700000-810000 \\ -840000+675000 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{x} \\ \mathrm{y} \end{array}\right]=\frac{1}{-11}\left[\begin{array}{l} -110000 \\ -165000 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{x} \\ \mathrm{y} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} \frac{-110000}{-11} \\ \frac{-165000}{-11} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex}{tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} \mathrm{x} \\ \mathrm{y} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 10000 \\ 15000 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Amount invested in the first bond = x = Rs 10000
Amount invested in the second bond = y = Rs 15000
Thus, the trust invested Rs 10000 in the first bond and Rs 15000 in the second bond.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} x \\ -1 \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, find x + y + z.
question_text: If {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} x \\ -1 \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , find x + y + z.
explanation: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} x \\ -1 \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} or {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} x \\ -y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} On comparing, we get x = 1, y = -2 and z = 1 Therefore, x + y + z = 1 - 2 + 1 = 0
solution: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} x \\ -1 \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} or {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} x \\ -y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} On comparing, we get x = 1, y = -2 and z = 1 Therefore, x + y + z = 1 - 2 + 1 = 0
solution_html:
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & y & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} x \\ -1 \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
or {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} x \\ -y \\ z \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 2 \\ 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}

Therefore, x + y + z = 1 - 2 + 1 = 0

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}\mathbf{A}=\left[\begin{array}{rr} 2 & 3 \\ 5 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} write A-1 in terms of A.
question_text: If {tex}\mathbf{A}=\left[\begin{array}{rr} 2 & 3 \\ 5 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} write A -1 in terms of A.
explanation: Here A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} 2 & 3 \\ 5 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Clearly, |A| = -4 - 15 = -19 We know that for 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} a & b \\ c & -d \end{array}\right]{/tex} We have A -1 = {tex}\frac{1}{|A|}{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} In our case A -1 = {tex}\frac{1}{|A|}{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} -2 & -3 \\ -5 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1}{19}{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} -2 & -3 \\ -5 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1}{19}{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} 2 & 3 \\ 5 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{19}{/tex} A {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A -1 = {tex}\frac{A}{19}{/tex}
solution: Here A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} 2 & 3 \\ 5 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Clearly, |A| = -4 - 15 = -19 We know that for 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} a & b \\ c & -d \end{array}\right]{/tex} We have A -1 = {tex}\frac{1}{|A|}{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} In our case A -1 = {tex}\frac{1}{|A|}{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} -2 & -3 \\ -5 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1}{19}{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} -2 & -3 \\ -5 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1}{19}{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} 2 & 3 \\ 5 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{19}{/tex} A {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A -1 = {tex}\frac{A}{19}{/tex}
solution_html:
Here
A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} 2 & 3 \\ 5 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} 
Clearly, |A| = -4 - 15 = -19
We know that for 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix
A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} a & b \\ c & -d \end{array}\right]{/tex} 
We have
A-1 = {tex}\frac{1}{|A|}{/tex}{tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} d & -b \\ -c & a \end{array}\right]{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} In our case
A-1 = {tex}\frac{1}{|A|}{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} -2 & -3 \\ -5 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{-1}{19}{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} -2 & -3 \\ -5 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} 
= {tex}\frac{-1}{19}{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} 2 & 3 \\ 5 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{19}{/tex} A
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A-1 = {tex}\frac{A}{19}{/tex}

question_html: Find the values of x and y if 2{tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{x} & {5} \\ {7} & {y-3}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{3} & {4} \\ {1} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{7} & {14} \\ {15} & {14}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_text: Find the values of x and y if 2 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{x} & {5} \\ {7} & {y-3}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{3} & {4} \\ {1} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{7} & {14} \\ {15} & {14}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
explanation: We have, 2 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{x} & {5} \\ {7} & {y-3}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{3} & {4} \\ {1} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}{7} & {14} \\ {15} & {14}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2 x} & {10} \\ {14} & {2 y-6}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{3} & {4} \\ {1} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{7} & {14} \\ {15} & {14}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2 x+3} & {14} \\ {15} & {2 y-4}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{7} & {14} \\ {15} & {14}\end{array}\right]{/tex} Comparing the corresponding elements from both sides, 2x + 3 = 7 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2 2y - 4 = 14 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2y = 18 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 9 Hence, x = 2, y = 9.
solution: We have, 2 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{x} & {5} \\ {7} & {y-3}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{3} & {4} \\ {1} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}{7} & {14} \\ {15} & {14}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2 x} & {10} \\ {14} & {2 y-6}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{3} & {4} \\ {1} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{7} & {14} \\ {15} & {14}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2 x+3} & {14} \\ {15} & {2 y-4}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{7} & {14} \\ {15} & {14}\end{array}\right]{/tex} Comparing the corresponding elements from both sides, 2x + 3 = 7 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2 2y - 4 = 14 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2y = 18 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 9 Hence, x = 2, y = 9.
solution_html:
We have,
2{tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{x} & {5} \\ {7} & {y-3}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{3} & {4} \\ {1} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{cc}{7} & {14} \\ {15} & {14}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2 x} & {10} \\ {14} & {2 y-6}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{3} & {4} \\ {1} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{7} & {14} \\ {15} & {14}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2 x+3} & {14} \\ {15} & {2 y-4}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{7} & {14} \\ {15} & {14}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
Comparing the corresponding elements from both sides,
2x + 3 = 7 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2
2y - 4 = 14 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2y = 18 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 9
Hence, x = 2, y = 9.

question_html: If {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 2 x } & { 3 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { - 3 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { l } { x } \\ { 3 } \end{array} \right] = 0{/tex}, find x.
question_text: If {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 2 x } & { 3 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { - 3 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { l } { x } \\ { 3 } \end{array} \right] = 0{/tex} , find x.

solution_html:
According to the question,
{tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 2 x } & { 3 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { c l } { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { - 3 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { l } { x } \\ { 3 } \end{array} \right] = 0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow [ 2 x - 9 \quad 4 x ] \left[ \begin{array} { l } { x } \\ { 3 } \end{array} \right] = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}[2x^2 - 9x + 12x] = [0]{/tex} 

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}2x^2 + 3x = 0{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x(2x + 3) = 0
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 0 or x = -3/2

1 } \\ { 2 } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right] + \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { a } & { 1 } \\ { b } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 + a } & { - 1 + 1 } \\ { 2 + b } & { - 1 - 1 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 + a } & { 0 } \\ { 2 + b } & { - 2 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow ( A + B ) ^ { 2 } = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 + a } & { 0 } \\ { 2 + b } & { - 2 } \end{array} \right] \cdot \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 + a } & { 0 } \\ { 2 + b } & { - 2 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 + a ^ { 2 } + 2 a } & { 0 } \\ { 2 + 2 a + b + a b - 4 - 2 b } & { 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { a ^ { 2 } + 2 a + 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 2 a - b + a b - 2 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} Also, A 2 + B 2 {tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 } & { - 1 } \\ { 2 } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right] \cdot \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 } & { - 1 } \\ { 2 } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right] + \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { a } & { 1 } \\ { b } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right] \cdot \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { a } & { 1 } \\ { b } & { - 1 } & { a - 1 } \\ { a b - b } & { b } \end{array} \right]{/tex} Therefore, on equating the corresponding elements, we get, a 2 + 2a + 1 = a 2 + b - 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2a - b = -2 ...(i) a - 1 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 1 ...(ii) 2a - 2 } \end{array} \right] \cdot \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 + a } & { 0 } \\ { 2 + b } & { - 2 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 + a ^ { 2 } + 2 a } & { 0 } \\ { 2 + 2 a + b + a b - 4 - 2 b } & { 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { a ^ { 2 } + 2 a + 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 2 a - b + a b - 2 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} Also, A 2 + B 2 {tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 } & { - 1 } & { a - 1 } \\ { a b - b } & { b } \end{array} \right]{/tex} Therefore, on equating the corresponding elements, we get, a 2 + 2a + 1 = a 2 + b - 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2a - b = -2 ...(i) a - 1 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 1 ...(ii) 2a - solution_html:
Given that, {tex}A = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 } & { - 1 } \\ { 2 } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right] \text { and } B = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { a } & { 1 } \\ { b } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}

Now, {tex}A + B = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 } & { - 1 } \\ { 2 } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right] + \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { a } & { 1 } \\ { b } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 + a } & { - 1 + 1 } \\ { 2 + b } & { - 1 - 1 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 + a } & { 0 } \\ { 2 + b } & { - 2 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow ( A + B ) ^ { 2 } = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 + a } & { 0 } \\ { 2 + b } & { - 2 } \end{array} \right] \cdot \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 + a } & { 0 } \\ { 2 + b } & { - 2 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 + a ^ { 2 } + 2 a } & { 0 } \\ { 2 + 2 a + b + a b - 4 - 2 b } & { 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { a ^ { 2 } + 2 a + 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 2 a - b + a b - 2 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
Also, A2 + B2 {tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 } & { - 1 } \\ { 2 } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right] \cdot \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 1 } & { - 1 } \\ { 2 } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right] + \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { a } & { 1 } \\ { b } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right] \cdot \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { a } & { 1 } \\ { b } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { r r } { - 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { - 1 } \end{array} \right] + \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { a ^ { 2 } + b } & { a - 1 } \\ { a b - b } & { b + 1 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { a ^ { 2 } + b - 1 } & { a - 1 } \\ { a b - b } & { b } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
Given, (A + B)2 = A2 + B2
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { a ^ { 2 } + 2 a + 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 2 a - b + a b - 2 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { a ^ { 2 } + b - 1 } & { a - 1 } \\ { a b - b } & { b } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
Therefore, on equating the corresponding elements, we get,
a2 + 2a + 1 = a2 + b - 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2a - b = -2 ...(i)
a - 1 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 1 ...(ii)
2a - b + ab - 2 = ab - b
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2a - 2 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} a = 1 ...(iii)
and b = 4 ...(iv)
Since, a = 1 and b = 4 also satisfy Eq. (i), therefore
a = 1 and b = 4

Now, let {tex}P = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( A + A ^ { \prime } \right){/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left\{ \left[ \begin{array} { r r r } { 2 } & { 4 } & { - 6 } \\ { 7 } & { 3 } & { 5 } \\ { 1 } & { - 2 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right] + \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 2 } & { 7 } & { 1 } \\ { 4 } & { 3 } & { - 2 } \\ { - 6 } & { 5 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right] \right\}{/tex}
{tex} = \frac{1}{2}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 4&{11}&{ - 5} \\ {11}&6&3 \\ { - 5}&3&8 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&{\frac{{11}}{2}}&{ - \frac{5}{2}} \\ {\frac{{11}}{2}}&3&{\frac{3}{2}} \\ { - \frac{5}{2}}&{\frac{3}{2}}&4 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
and {tex}Q = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( A - A ^ { \prime } \right){/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left\{ \left[ \begin{array} { r r r } { 2 } & { 4 } & { - 6 } \\ { 7 } & { 3 } & { 5 } \\ { 1 } & { - 2 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right] - \left[ \begin{array} { r r r } { 2 } & { 7 } & { 1 } \\ { 4 } & { 3 } & { - 2 } \\ { - 6 } & { 5 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right] \right\}{/tex}
{tex}= \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 0 } & { - 3 } & { - 7 } \\ { 3 } & { 0 } & { 7 } \\ { 7 } & { - 7 } & { 0 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 0 } & { - \frac { 3 } { 2 } } & { - \frac { 7 } { 2 } } \\ { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } & { 0 } & { \frac { 7 } { 2 } } \\ { \frac { 7 } { 2 } } & { - \frac { 7 } { 2 } } & { 0 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
Obviously, here {tex}P' = \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 2 } & { \frac { 11 } { 2 } } & { \frac { - 5 } { 2 } } \\ { \frac { 11 } { 2 } } & { 3 } & { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } \\ { \frac { - 5 } { 2 } } & { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } & { 4 } \end{array} \right] = P{/tex}
and {tex}{Q^\prime } = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{\frac{3}{2}}&{\frac{7}{2}} \\ {\frac{{ - 3}}{2}}&0&{\frac{{ - 7}}{2}} \\ {\frac{{ - 7}}{2}}&{\frac{7}{2}}&0 \end{array}} \right] = - Q{/tex}
Therefore, P is symmetric matrix and Q is a skew-symmetric matrix.
Now, {tex}P + Q = \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( A + A ^ { \prime } \right) + \frac { 1 } { 2 } \left( A - A ^ { \prime } \right){/tex}
{tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { r r r } { 2 } & { \frac { 11 } { 2 } } & { - \frac { 5 } { 2 } } \\ { \frac { 11 } { 2 } } & { 3 } & { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } \\ { - \frac { 5 } { 2 } } & { \frac { 3 } { 2 } } & { 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex} + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&{ - \frac{3}{2}}&{ - \frac{7}{2}} \\ {\frac{3}{2}}&0&{\frac{7}{2}} \\ {\frac{7}{2}}&{ - \frac{7}{2}}&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { r r r } { 2 } & { 4 } & { - 6 } \\ { 7 } & { 3 } & { 5 } \\ { 1 } & { - 2 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right] = A{/tex}
Therefore, matrix A is expressed as the sum of symmetric matrix and skew-symmetric matrix.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 4 \\ 3 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} -2 & 5 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, then find (3A - B).
question_text: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 4 \\ 3 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} -2 & 5 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , then find (3A - B).
explanation: We have, by using matrices A and B 3A - B = {tex}3\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {4} \\ {3} & {2} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-2} & {5} \\ {3} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {3 \times 2} & {3 \times 4} \\ {3 \times 3} & {3 \times 2} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-2} & {5} \\ {3} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {6} & {12} \\ {9} & {6} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-2} & {5} \\ {3} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {6+2} & {12-5} \\ {9-3} & {6-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {8} & {7} \\ {6} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution: We have, by using matrices A and B 3A - B = {tex}3\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {4} \\ {3} & {2} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-2} & {5} \\ {3} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {3 \times 2} & {3 \times 4} \\ {3 \times 3} & {3 \times 2} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-2} & {5} \\ {3} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {6} & {12} \\ {9} & {6} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-2} & {5} \\ {3} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {6+2} & {12-5} \\ {9-3} & {6-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {8} & {7} \\ {6} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, by using matrices A and B
3A - B = {tex}3\left[\begin{array}{cc} {2} & {4} \\ {3} & {2} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-2} & {5} \\ {3} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} 
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {3 \times 2} & {3 \times 4} \\ {3 \times 3} & {3 \times 2} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-2} & {5} \\ {3} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} 
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {6} & {12} \\ {9} & {6} \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} {-2} & {5} \\ {3} & {4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} 
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} {6+2} & {12-5} \\ {9-3} & {6-4} \end{array}\right]{/tex} 
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} {8} & {7} \\ {6} & {2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}

question_html:
In a parliament election, a political party hired a public relations firm to promote its candidates in three ways - telephone, house calls and letters. The cost per contact (in paise) is given in matrix A as
{tex}A = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 140 } \\ { 200 } \\ { 150 } \end{array} \right] \begin{array} { c } { \text { Telephone } } \\ { \text { House call } } \\ { \text { Letters } } \end{array}{/tex}
The number of contacts of each type made in two cities X and Y is given in the matrix B as
{tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {} \\ {B = } \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {Telephone}&{House\;Call}&{Letters} \end{array}} \\ {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1000}&{500}&{5000} \\ {3000}&{1000}&{10000} \end{array}} \right]} \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {} \\ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {City\;X} \\ {City\;Y} \end{array}} \end{array}{/tex}
Find the total amount spent by the party in the two cities.
What should one consider before casting his/her vote - party's promotional activity or their social activties?
question_text: In a parliament election, a political party hired a public relations firm to promote its candidates in three ways - telephone, house calls and letters. The cost per contact (in paise) is given in matrix A as {tex}A = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 140 } \\ { 200 } \\ { 150 } \end{array} \right] \begin{array} { c } { \text { Telephone } } \\ { \text { House call } } \\ { \text { Letters } } \end{array}{/tex} The number of contacts of each type made in two cities X and Y is given in the matrix B as {tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {} \\ {B = } \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {Telephone}&{House\;Call}&{Letters} \end{array}} \\ {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1000}&{500}&{5000} \\ {3000}&{1000}&{10000} \end{array}} \right]} \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {} \\ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {City\;X} \\ {City\;Y} \end{array}} \end{array}{/tex} Find the total amount spent by the party in the two cities. What should one consider before casting his/her vote - party's promotional activity or their social activties?
explanation: Given, the cost per contact (in paise) in matrix A as {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {140} \\ {200} \\ {150} \end{array}} \right]\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {Telephone} \\ {House\;call} \\ {Letters} \end{array}{/tex} and the number of contact of each type in matrix B as {tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {} \\ {B = } \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {} \\ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {City\;X} \\ {City\;Y} \end{array}} \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {Telephone}&{House\;Call}&{Letters} \end{array}} \\ {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1000}&{500}&{5000} \\ {3000}&{1000}&{10000} \end{array}} \right]} \end{array}{/tex} Clearly, the amount spent by each party in the two cities is given by BA {tex}= \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {City\;X} \\ {City\;Y} \end{array}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1000}&{500}&{5000} \\ {3000}&{1000}&{10000} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {140} \\ {200} \\ {150} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {City\;X} \\ {City\;Y} \end{array}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {140000 + 100000 + 750000} \\ {420000 + 200000 + 1500000} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}=\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {City\;X} \\ {City\;Y} \end{array}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {990000} \\ {2120000} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Total amount spent by the party in the cities X and Y are paise 990000 ( or Rs. 9900) and paise 2120000 (or Rs. 21200), respectively. Everone must consider the social activities of the party before casting his/her vote.
solution: Given, the cost per contact (in paise) in matrix A as {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {140} \\ {200} \\ {150} \end{array}} \right]\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {Telephone} \\ {House\;call} \\ {Letters} \end{array}{/tex} and the number of contact of each type in matrix B as {tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {} \\ {B = } \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {} \\ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {City\;X} \\ {City\;Y} \end{array}} \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {Telephone}&{House\;Call}&{Letters} \end{array}} \\ {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1000}&{500}&{5000} \\ {3000}&{1000}&{10000} \end{array}} \right]} \end{array}{/tex} Clearly, the amount spent by each party in the two cities is given by BA {tex}= \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {City\;X} \\ {City\;Y} \end{array}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1000}&{500}&{5000} \\ {3000}&{1000}&{10000} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {140} \\ {200} \\ {150} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {City\;X} \\ {City\;Y} \end{array}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {140000 + 100000 + 750000} \\ {420000 + 200000 + 1500000} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}=\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {City\;X} \\ {City\;Y} \end{array}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {990000} \\ {2120000} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Total amount spent by the party in the cities X and Y are paise 990000 ( or Rs. 9900) and paise 2120000 (or Rs. 21200), respectively. Everone must consider the social activities of the party before casting his/her vote.
solution_html:
Given, the cost per contact (in paise) in matrix A
as {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {140} \\ {200} \\ {150} \end{array}} \right]\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {Telephone} \\ {House\;call} \\ {Letters} \end{array}{/tex}
and the number of contact of each type in matrix B as
{tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {} \\ {B = } \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {} \\ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {City\;X} \\ {City\;Y} \end{array}} \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {Telephone}&{House\;Call}&{Letters} \end{array}} \\ {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1000}&{500}&{5000} \\ {3000}&{1000}&{10000} \end{array}} \right]} \end{array}{/tex}
Clearly, the amount spent by each party in the two cities is given by BA
{tex}= \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {City\;X} \\ {City\;Y} \end{array}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1000}&{500}&{5000} \\ {3000}&{1000}&{10000} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {140} \\ {200} \\ {150} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {City\;X} \\ {City\;Y} \end{array}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {140000 + 100000 + 750000} \\ {420000 + 200000 + 1500000} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}=\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {City\;X} \\ {City\;Y} \end{array}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {990000} \\ {2120000} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Total amount spent by the party in the cities X and Y are paise 990000 ( or Rs. 9900) and paise 2120000 (or Rs. 21200), respectively.
Everone must consider the social activities of the party before casting his/her vote.

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
A trust fund has Rs. 35000 is to be invested in two different types of bonds. The first bond pays 8% interest per annum which will be given to orphanage and second bond pays 10% interest per annum which will be given to an NGO (Cancer Ad Society).
Using matrix multiplication, determine how to divide Rs. 35000 among two types of bonds, if the trust fund obtains an annual total interest of Rs. 3200. What are the values reﬂected in this question?
question_text: A trust fund has Rs. 35000 is to be invested in two different types of bonds. The first bond pays 8% interest per annum which will be given to orphanage and second bond pays 10% interest per annum which will be given to an NGO (Cancer Ad Society). Using matrix multiplication, determine how to divide Rs. 35000 among two types of bonds, if the trust fund obtains an annual total interest of Rs. 3200. What are the values reﬂected in this question?
explanation: Let the amount invested in 1st bond be Rs. x, then Rs. 35000 - x will be invested in 2nd bond. Let matrix A represent the amount invested in two bonds, then A can be written as A = [x 35000 - x] Also, let matrix B represent the rate of interest field on both types of bonds, then B can be written as {tex}B = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 8 \% } \\ { 10 \% } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { \frac { 8 } { 100 } } \\ { \frac { 10 } { 100 } } \end{array} \right]{/tex} Now, according to question, {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { x } & { 35000 - x } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { c } { \frac { 8 } { 100 } } \\ { \frac { 10 } { 100 } } \end{array} \right] = [ 3200 ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ \frac { 8 x } { 100 } + \frac { 350000 - 10 x } { 100 } \right] = [ 3200 ]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { 8 x + 350000 - 10 x } { 100 } = 3200{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 350000 - 2x = 320000 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 350000 - 320000 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 30000 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 15000 Thus, the amount invested in 2nd bond = 35000 - 15000 = Rs. 20000 The values reﬂected in this questions, are spirit of investment, helping the orphans living in the society, and helping patient suffering from cancer.
solution: Let the amount invested in 1st bond be Rs. x, then Rs. 35000 - x will be invested in 2nd bond. Let matrix A represent the amount invested in two bonds, then A can be written as A = [x 35000 - x] Also, let matrix B represent the rate of interest field on both types of bonds, then B can be written as {tex}B = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 8 \% } \\ { 10 \% } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { \frac { 8 } { 100 } } \\ { \frac { 10 } { 100 } } \end{array} \right]{/tex} Now, according to question, {tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { x } & { 35000 - x } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { c } { \frac { 8 } { 100 } } \\ { \frac { 10 } { 100 } } \end{array} \right] = [ 3200 ]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ \frac { 8 x } { 100 } + \frac { 350000 - 10 x } { 100 } \right] = [ 3200 ]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \frac { 8 x + 350000 - 10 x } { 100 } = 3200{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 350000 - 2x = 320000 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 350000 - 320000 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 30000 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 15000 Thus, the amount invested in 2nd bond = 35000 - 15000 = Rs. 20000 The values reﬂected in this questions, are spirit of investment, helping the orphans living in the society, and helping patient suffering from cancer.
solution_html:
Let the amount invested in 1st bond be Rs. x, then Rs. 35000 - x will be invested in 2nd bond. Let matrix A represent the amount invested in two bonds, then A can be written as
A = [x  35000 - x]
Also, let matrix B represent the rate of interest field on both types of bonds, then B can be written as
{tex}B = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 8 \% } \\ { 10 \% } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { \frac { 8 } { 100 } } \\ { \frac { 10 } { 100 } } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
Now, according to question,
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { x } & { 35000 - x } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { c } { \frac { 8 } { 100 } } \\ { \frac { 10 } { 100 } } \end{array} \right] = [ 3200 ]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ \frac { 8 x } { 100 } + \frac { 350000 - 10 x } { 100 } \right] = [ 3200 ]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \frac { 8 x + 350000 - 10 x } { 100 } = 3200{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 350000 - 2x = 320000 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 350000 - 320000
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 30000 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 15000
Thus, the amount invested in 2nd bond = 35000 - 15000 = Rs. 20000
The values reﬂected in this questions, are spirit of investment, helping the orphans living in the society, and helping patient suffering from cancer.

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
Three schools A, B and C organised a mela for collecting funds for helping the rehabilitation of ﬂood victims. They sold handmade fans, mats and plates from recycled material at a cost of Rs. 25, ₹ 100 and ₹ 50 each. The number of articles sold are given below

Article/School A B C
Hand-fans 40 25 35
Mats 50 40 50
Plates 20 30 40

Find the funds collected by each school separately by selling the above articles. Also, find the total funds collected for the purpose. Write one value generated by the above situation.
question_text: Three schools A, B and C organised a mela for collecting funds for helping the rehabilitation of ﬂood victims. They sold handmade fans, mats and plates from recycled material at a cost of Rs. 25, ₹ 100 and ₹ 50 each. The number of articles sold are given below Article/School A B C Hand-fans 40 25 35 Mats 50 40 50 Plates 20 30 40 Find the funds collected by each school separately by selling the above articles. Also, find the total funds collected for the purpose. Write one value generated by the above situation.
explanation: Let A be the matrix of number of articles sold by different schools and B be the matrix of cost of articles. Then, matrices A and B can be represented as {tex}A = \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {} \\ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {Handmadefans} \\ {Mats} \\ {Plates} \end{array}} \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} A&B&C \end{array}} \\ {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {40}&{25}&{35} \\ {50}&{40}&{50} \\ {20}&{30}&{40} \end{array}} \right]} \end{array}{/tex} {tex}B = \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {} \\ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {Handmade\,fans} \\ {Mats} \\ {Plates} \end{array}} \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{\text{Cost}}\,{\text{Rs}}{\text{.}}} \\ {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {25} \\ {100} \\ {50} \end{array}} \right]} \end{array}{/tex} Now, the funds collected by each school separately can be represented as {tex}A'B = \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {School\,A} \\ {School\,B} \\ {School\,C} \end{array}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {40}&{50}&{20} \\ {25}&{40}&{30} \\ {35}&{50}&{40} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {25} \\ {100} \\ {50} \end{array}} \right]\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {Handmade\,fans} \\ {Mats} \\ {Plates} \end{array}{/tex} {tex} = \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {School\,A} \\ {School\,B} \\ {School\,C} \end{array}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {40 \times 25 + 50 \times 100 + 20 \times 50} \\ {25 \times 25 + 40 \times 100 + 30 \times 50} \\ {35 \times 25 + 50 \times 100 + 40 \times 50} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} [multiplying row by columns] {tex} = \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {School\,A} \\ {School\,B} \\ {School\,C} \end{array}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1000 + 5000 + 1000} \\ {625 + 4000 + 1500} \\ {875 + 5000 + 2000} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {School\,A} \\ {School\,B} \\ {School\,C} \end{array}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {7000} \\ {6125} \\ {7875} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Now, the total funds collected for the purpose = 7000 + 6125 + 7875 = Rs. 21000 The one of the value generated by the above situation is 'humanty'.
solution: Let A be the matrix of number of articles sold by different schools and B be the matrix of cost of articles. Then, matrices A and B can be represented as {tex}A = \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {} \\ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {Handmadefans} \\ {Mats} \\ {Plates} \end{array}} \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} A&B&C \end{array}} \\ {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {40}&{25}&{35} \\ {50}&{40}&{50} \\ {20}&{30}&{40} \end{array}} \right]} \end{array}{/tex} {tex}B = \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {} \\ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {Handmade\,fans} \\ {Mats} \\ {Plates} \end{array}} \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{\text{Cost}}\,{\text{Rs}}{\text{.}}} \\ {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {25} \\ {100} \\ {50} \end{array}} \right]} \end{array}{/tex} Now, the funds collected by each school separately can be represented as {tex}A'B = \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {School\,A} \\ {School\,B} \\ {School\,C} \end{array}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {40}&{50}&{20} \\ {25}&{40}&{30} \\ {35}&{50}&{40} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {25} \\ {100} \\ {50} \end{array}} \right]\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {Handmade\,fans} \\ {Mats} \\ {Plates} \end{array}{/tex} {tex} = \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {School\,A} \\ {School\,B} \\ {School\,C} \end{array}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {40 \times 25 + 50 \times 100 + 20 \times 50} \\ {25 \times 25 + 40 \times 100 + 30 \times 50} \\ {35 \times 25 + 50 \times 100 + 40 \times 50} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} [multiplying row by columns] {tex} = \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {School\,A} \\ {School\,B} \\ {School\,C} \end{array}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1000 + 5000 + 1000} \\ {625 + 4000 + 1500} \\ {875 + 5000 + 2000} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {School\,A} \\ {School\,B} \\ {School\,C} \end{array}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {7000} \\ {6125} \\ {7875} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Now, the total funds collected for the purpose = 7000 + 6125 + 7875 = Rs. 21000 The one of the value generated by the above situation is 'humanty'.
solution_html:
Let A be the matrix of number of articles sold by different schools and B be the matrix of cost of articles.
Then, matrices A and B can be represented as
{tex}A = \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {} \\ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {Handmadefans} \\ {Mats} \\ {Plates} \end{array}} \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} A&B&C \end{array}} \\ {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {40}&{25}&{35} \\ {50}&{40}&{50} \\ {20}&{30}&{40} \end{array}} \right]} \end{array}{/tex}
{tex}B = \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {} \\ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {Handmade\,fans} \\ {Mats} \\ {Plates} \end{array}} \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{\text{Cost}}\,{\text{Rs}}{\text{.}}} \\ {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {25} \\ {100} \\ {50} \end{array}} \right]} \end{array}{/tex}
Now, the funds collected by each school separately can be represented as
{tex}A'B = \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {School\,A} \\ {School\,B} \\ {School\,C} \end{array}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {40}&{50}&{20} \\ {25}&{40}&{30} \\ {35}&{50}&{40} \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {25} \\ {100} \\ {50} \end{array}} \right]\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {Handmade\,fans} \\ {Mats} \\ {Plates} \end{array}{/tex}
{tex} = \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {School\,A} \\ {School\,B} \\ {School\,C} \end{array}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {40 \times 25 + 50 \times 100 + 20 \times 50} \\ {25 \times 25 + 40 \times 100 + 30 \times 50} \\ {35 \times 25 + 50 \times 100 + 40 \times 50} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} [multiplying row by columns]
{tex} = \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {School\,A} \\ {School\,B} \\ {School\,C} \end{array}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {1000 + 5000 + 1000} \\ {625 + 4000 + 1500} \\ {875 + 5000 + 2000} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {School\,A} \\ {School\,B} \\ {School\,C} \end{array}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {7000} \\ {6125} \\ {7875} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Now, the total funds collected for the purpose
= 7000 + 6125 + 7875 = Rs. 21000
The one of the value generated by the above situation is 'humanty'.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the inverse of the matrix A {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 3 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 & 3 \\ 1 & 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_text: Find the inverse of the matrix A {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 3 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 & 3 \\ 1 & 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
explanation: Let {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 3 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 & 3 \\ 1 & 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] A{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 3 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] A{/tex} (applying {tex}R_{2} \leftrightarrow R_{2}-R_{1}, R_{3} \leftrightarrow R_{3}-R_{1}{/tex} ) {tex}\Rightarrow \left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & -3 & 0 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]A{/tex} (applying {tex}R_{1} \leftrightarrow R_{1}-3 R_{2}{/tex} ) {tex}\Rightarrow \left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & -3 & -3 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] A{/tex} (applying {tex}R_{1} \leftrightarrow R_{1}-3 R_{3}{/tex} ) Thus, {tex}A^{-1}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & -3 & -3 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution: Let {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 3 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 & 3 \\ 1 & 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] A{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 3 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] A{/tex} (applying {tex}R_{2} \leftrightarrow R_{2}-R_{1}, R_{3} \leftrightarrow R_{3}-R_{1}{/tex} ) {tex}\Rightarrow \left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & -3 & 0 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]A{/tex} (applying {tex}R_{1} \leftrightarrow R_{1}-3 R_{2}{/tex} ) {tex}\Rightarrow \left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & -3 & -3 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] A{/tex} (applying {tex}R_{1} \leftrightarrow R_{1}-3 R_{3}{/tex} ) Thus, {tex}A^{-1}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & -3 & -3 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Let
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 3 & 3 \\ 1 & 4 & 3 \\ 1 & 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] A{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 3 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] A{/tex} (applying {tex}R_{2} \leftrightarrow R_{2}-R_{1}, R_{3} \leftrightarrow R_{3}-R_{1}{/tex})
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 3 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & -3 & 0 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]A{/tex} (applying {tex}R_{1} \leftrightarrow R_{1}-3 R_{2}{/tex})
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & -3 & -3 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right] A{/tex} (applying {tex}R_{1} \leftrightarrow R_{1}-3 R_{3}{/tex})
Thus, {tex}A^{-1}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 7 & -3 & -3 \\ -1 & 1 & 0 \\ -1 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: For the matrix A = {tex}\left(\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & -1 & 1 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \\ 1 & -1 & 2 \end{array}\right){/tex}, show that A2 - 5A + 4I = O. Hence find A-1.
question_text: For the matrix A = {tex}\left(\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & -1 & 1 \\ -1 & 2 & -1 \\ 1 & -1 & 2 \end{array}\right){/tex} , show that A 2 - 5A + 4I = O. Hence find A -1 .
explanation: We know that {tex}A^{2}-5 A+4 I=A A-5 A+4 I{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & -1 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & -1 & 0 \end{array}\right]-5\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & -1 & 0 \end{array}\right]+4\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 4+0+1 & 0+0+(-1) & 2+0+0 \\ 4+2+3 & 0+1-3 & 2+3+0 \\ 2-2+0 & 0-1+0 & 1-3+0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -10 & 0 & -5 \\ -10 & -5 & -15 \\ -5 & 5 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 4 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & 2 \\ 9 & -2 & 5 \\ 0 & -1 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -6 & 0 & -5 \\ -10 & -1 & -15 \\ -5 & 5 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -1 & -1 & -3 \\ -1 & -3 & -10 \\ -5 & +4 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = Y Y + X =0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} X = -Y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} X = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & \frac{1}{3} & 3 \\ 1 & 3 & 10 \\ 5 & -4 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution: We know that {tex}A^{2}-5 A+4 I=A A-5 A+4 I{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & -1 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & -1 & 0 \end{array}\right]-5\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & -1 & 0 \end{array}\right]+4\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 4+0+1 & 0+0+(-1) & 2+0+0 \\ 4+2+3 & 0+1-3 & 2+3+0 \\ 2-2+0 & 0-1+0 & 1-3+0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -10 & 0 & -5 \\ -10 & -5 & -15 \\ -5 & 5 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 4 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & 2 \\ 9 & -2 & 5 \\ 0 & -1 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -6 & 0 & -5 \\ -10 & -1 & -15 \\ -5 & 5 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -1 & -1 & -3 \\ -1 & -3 & -10 \\ -5 & +4 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = Y Y + X =0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} X = -Y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} X = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & \frac{1}{3} & 3 \\ 1 & 3 & 10 \\ 5 & -4 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
We know that
{tex}A^{2}-5 A+4 I=A A-5 A+4 I{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & -1 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & -1 & 0 \end{array}\right]-5\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & -1 & 0 \end{array}\right]+4\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 4+0+1 & 0+0+(-1) & 2+0+0 \\ 4+2+3 & 0+1-3 & 2+3+0 \\ 2-2+0 & 0-1+0 & 1-3+0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}+ {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -10 & 0 & -5 \\ -10 & -5 & -15 \\ -5 & 5 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 4 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 4 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & 2 \\ 9 & -2 & 5 \\ 0 & -1 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}+ {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -6 & 0 & -5 \\ -10 & -1 & -15 \\ -5 & 5 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -1 & -1 & -3 \\ -1 & -3 & -10 \\ -5 & +4 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = Y
Y + X =0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} X = -Y
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} X = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & \frac{1}{3} & 3 \\ 1 & 3 & 10 \\ 5 & -4 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}\left(\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & -1 & 0 \end{array}\right){/tex}, find A2 - 5 A + 16 I
question_text: If {tex}\left(\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & -1 & 0 \end{array}\right){/tex} , find A 2 - 5 A + 16 I
explanation: Finding A 2 = AA {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & -1 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & -1 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2(2)+0(2)+1(1) & 2(0)+0(1)+1(-1) & 2(1)+0(3)+1(0) \\ 2(2)+1(2)+3(1) & 2(0)+1(1)+3(-1) & 2(1)+1(3)+3(0) \\ 1(2)+-1(2)+0(1) & 1(0)+-1(1)+0(-1) & 1(1)+-1(3)+0(0) \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 4+0+1 & 0+0-1 & 2+0+0 \\ 4+2+3 & 0+1-3 & 2+3+0 \\ 2-2+0 & 0-1+0 & 1-3+0 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & 2 \\ 9 & -2 & 5 \\ 0 & -1 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now calculating A 2 - 5A+ 6I {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & 2 \\ 9 & -2 & 5 \\ 0 & -1 & -2 \end{array}\right]-5\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & -1 & 0 \end{array}\right]+6\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & 2 \\ 9 & -2 & 5 \\ 0 & -1 & -2 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 \times 5 & 0 \times 5 & 1 \times 5 \\ 2 \times 5 & 1 \times 5 & 3 \times 5 \\ 1 \times 5 & -1 \times 5 & 0 \times 5 \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 \times 6 & 0 \times 6 & 0 \times 6 \\ 0 \times 6 & 1 \times 6 & 0 \times 6 \\ 0 \times 6 & 0 \times 6 & 1 \times 6 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & 2 \\ 9 & -2 & 5 \\ 0 & -1 & -2 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 10 & 0 & 5 \\ 10 & 5 & 15 \\ 5 & -5 & 0 \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 6 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 6 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 6 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5-10+6 & -1-0+0 & 2-5+0 \\ 9-10+0 & -2-5+6 & 5-15+0 \\ 0-5+0 & -1+5+0 & -2-0+6 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -1 & -3 \\ -1 & -1 & -10 \\ -5 & 4 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution: Finding A 2 = AA {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & -1 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & -1 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2(2)+0(2)+1(1) & 2(0)+0(1)+1(-1) & 2(1)+0(3)+1(0) \\ 2(2)+1(2)+3(1) & 2(0)+1(1)+3(-1) & 2(1)+1(3)+3(0) \\ 1(2)+-1(2)+0(1) & 1(0)+-1(1)+0(-1) & 1(1)+-1(3)+0(0) \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 4+0+1 & 0+0-1 & 2+0+0 \\ 4+2+3 & 0+1-3 & 2+3+0 \\ 2-2+0 & 0-1+0 & 1-3+0 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & 2 \\ 9 & -2 & 5 \\ 0 & -1 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now calculating A 2 - 5A+ 6I {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & 2 \\ 9 & -2 & 5 \\ 0 & -1 & -2 \end{array}\right]-5\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & -1 & 0 \end{array}\right]+6\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & 2 \\ 9 & -2 & 5 \\ 0 & -1 & -2 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 \times 5 & 0 \times 5 & 1 \times 5 \\ 2 \times 5 & 1 \times 5 & 3 \times 5 \\ 1 \times 5 & -1 \times 5 & 0 \times 5 \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 \times 6 & 0 \times 6 & 0 \times 6 \\ 0 \times 6 & 1 \times 6 & 0 \times 6 \\ 0 \times 6 & 0 \times 6 & 1 \times 6 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & 2 \\ 9 & -2 & 5 \\ 0 & -1 & -2 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 10 & 0 & 5 \\ 10 & 5 & 15 \\ 5 & -5 & 0 \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 6 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 6 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 6 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5-10+6 & -1-0+0 & 2-5+0 \\ 9-10+0 & -2-5+6 & 5-15+0 \\ 0-5+0 & -1+5+0 & -2-0+6 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -1 & -3 \\ -1 & -1 & -10 \\ -5 & 4 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Finding 
A2 = AA
{tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & -1 & 0 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & -1 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2(2)+0(2)+1(1) & 2(0)+0(1)+1(-1) & 2(1)+0(3)+1(0) \\ 2(2)+1(2)+3(1) & 2(0)+1(1)+3(-1) & 2(1)+1(3)+3(0) \\ 1(2)+-1(2)+0(1) & 1(0)+-1(1)+0(-1) & 1(1)+-1(3)+0(0) \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 4+0+1 & 0+0-1 & 2+0+0 \\ 4+2+3 & 0+1-3 & 2+3+0 \\ 2-2+0 & 0-1+0 & 1-3+0 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & 2 \\ 9 & -2 & 5 \\ 0 & -1 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now calculating 
A2 - 5A+ 6I
{tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & 2 \\ 9 & -2 & 5 \\ 0 & -1 & -2 \end{array}\right]-5\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 & 0 & 1 \\ 2 & 1 & 3 \\ 1 & -1 & 0 \end{array}\right]+6\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & 2 \\ 9 & -2 & 5 \\ 0 & -1 & -2 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 2 \times 5 & 0 \times 5 & 1 \times 5 \\ 2 \times 5 & 1 \times 5 & 3 \times 5 \\ 1 \times 5 & -1 \times 5 & 0 \times 5 \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 \times 6 & 0 \times 6 & 0 \times 6 \\ 0 \times 6 & 1 \times 6 & 0 \times 6 \\ 0 \times 6 & 0 \times 6 & 1 \times 6 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & -1 & 2 \\ 9 & -2 & 5 \\ 0 & -1 & -2 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 10 & 0 & 5 \\ 10 & 5 & 15 \\ 5 & -5 & 0 \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 6 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 6 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 6 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5-10+6 & -1-0+0 & 2-5+0 \\ 9-10+0 & -2-5+6 & 5-15+0 \\ 0-5+0 & -1+5+0 & -2-0+6 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 & -1 & -3 \\ -1 & -1 & -10 \\ -5 & 4 & 4 \end{array}\right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
To promote the making of toilets for women, an organisation tried to generate awareness through (i) house calls (ii) letters and (iii) announcements.
The cost for each mode per attempt is given below:

  1. Rs. 50
  2. Rs. 20
  3. Rs. 40

The number of attempts made in three vllages X, Y and Z are given below:

  (i) (ii) (iii)
X 400 300 100
Y 300 250 75
Z 500 400 150

Find the total cost incurred by the organisation for the three villages separately, using matrices.
Write one value generated by the organisation in the society.
question_text: To promote the making of toilets for women, an organisation tried to generate awareness through (i) house calls (ii) letters and (iii) announcements. The cost for each mode per attempt is given below: Rs. 50 Rs. 20 Rs. 40 The number of attempts made in three vllages X, Y and Z are given below: (i) (ii) (iii) X 400 300 100 Y 300 250 75 Z 500 400 150 Find the total cost incurred by the organisation for the three villages separately, using matrices. Write one value generated by the organisation in the society.
explanation: Let A be the matrix that represent the number of attempts made in three villages X, Y and Z and let B be the matrix that represent the cost for each mode per attempt. Then, the matrices A and B can be represented as A = {tex} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {} \\ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{array}} \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {Hou.}&{C\,L}&{Anno.} \end{array}} \\ {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {400}&{300}&{100} \\ {300}&{250}&7 \\ {500}&{400}&{150} \end{array}} \right]} \end{array}{/tex} and B = {tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {}&{{\text{Cost Rs}}{\text{.}}} \\ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {House} \\ {Call\;Latters} \\ {Announcement} \end{array}}&{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {50} \\ {20} \\ {40} \end{array}} \right]} \end{array}{/tex} Now, the cost incurred by the organization for thee villages can be represented as AB = {tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {}&{} \\ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{array}}&{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {400}&{300}&{100} \\ {300}&{250}&{75} \\ {500}&{400}&{150} \end{array}} \right]} \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{\text{Cost Rs}}{\text{.}}} \\ {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {50} \\ {20} \\ {40} \end{array}} \right]} \end{array}{/tex} {tex} = \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{array}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {400 \times 50 + 300 \times 20 + 100 \times 40} \\ {300 \times 50 + 250 \times 20 + 75 \times 40} \\ {500 \times 50 + 400 \times 20 + 150 \times 40} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{array}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {20000 + 6000 + 4000} \\ {15000 + 5000 + 3000} \\ {25000 + 8000 + 6000} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{array}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {30000} \\ {23000} \\ {39000} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Thus, the total cost incurred by the organisation for the three villages X, Y and Z separately are Rs. 30000, Rs. 23000 and Rs. 39000, respectively. The value generated by the organisation in the society is cleanliness.
solution: Let A be the matrix that represent the number of attempts made in three villages X, Y and Z and let B be the matrix that represent the cost for each mode per attempt. Then, the matrices A and B can be represented as A = {tex} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {} \\ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{array}} \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {Hou.}&{C\,L}&{Anno.} \end{array}} \\ {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {400}&{300}&{100} \\ {300}&{250}&7 \\ {500}&{400}&{150} \end{array}} \right]} \end{array}{/tex} and B = {tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {}&{{\text{Cost Rs}}{\text{.}}} \\ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {House} \\ {Call\;Latters} \\ {Announcement} \end{array}}&{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {50} \\ {20} \\ {40} \end{array}} \right]} \end{array}{/tex} Now, the cost incurred by the organization for thee villages can be represented as AB = {tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {}&{} \\ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{array}}&{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {400}&{300}&{100} \\ {300}&{250}&{75} \\ {500}&{400}&{150} \end{array}} \right]} \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{\text{Cost Rs}}{\text{.}}} \\ {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {50} \\ {20} \\ {40} \end{array}} \right]} \end{array}{/tex} {tex} = \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{array}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {400 \times 50 + 300 \times 20 + 100 \times 40} \\ {300 \times 50 + 250 \times 20 + 75 \times 40} \\ {500 \times 50 + 400 \times 20 + 150 \times 40} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{array}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {20000 + 6000 + 4000} \\ {15000 + 5000 + 3000} \\ {25000 + 8000 + 6000} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{array}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {30000} \\ {23000} \\ {39000} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Thus, the total cost incurred by the organisation for the three villages X, Y and Z separately are Rs. 30000, Rs. 23000 and Rs. 39000, respectively. The value generated by the organisation in the society is cleanliness.
solution_html:
Let A be the matrix that represent the number of attempts made in three villages X, Y and Z and let B be the matrix that represent the cost for each mode per attempt.

Then, the matrices A and B can be represented as
A = {tex} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {} \\ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{array}} \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {Hou.}&{C\,L}&{Anno.} \end{array}} \\ {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {400}&{300}&{100} \\ {300}&{250}&7 \\ {500}&{400}&{150} \end{array}} \right]} \end{array}{/tex} and B = {tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {}&{{\text{Cost Rs}}{\text{.}}} \\ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {House} \\ {Call\;Latters} \\ {Announcement} \end{array}}&{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {50} \\ {20} \\ {40} \end{array}} \right]} \end{array}{/tex}
Now, the cost incurred by the organization for thee villages can be represented as
AB = {tex}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {}&{} \\ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{array}}&{\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {400}&{300}&{100} \\ {300}&{250}&{75} \\ {500}&{400}&{150} \end{array}} \right]} \end{array}\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {{\text{Cost Rs}}{\text{.}}} \\ {\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {50} \\ {20} \\ {40} \end{array}} \right]} \end{array}{/tex}
{tex} = \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{array}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {400 \times 50 + 300 \times 20 + 100 \times 40} \\ {300 \times 50 + 250 \times 20 + 75 \times 40} \\ {500 \times 50 + 400 \times 20 + 150 \times 40} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{array}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {20000 + 6000 + 4000} \\ {15000 + 5000 + 3000} \\ {25000 + 8000 + 6000} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} X \\ Y \\ Z \end{array}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {30000} \\ {23000} \\ {39000} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Thus, the total cost incurred by the organisation for the three villages X, Y and Z separately are Rs. 30000, Rs. 23000 and Rs. 39000, respectively.
The value generated by the organisation in the society is cleanliness.

question_html: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {6} & {7} \\ {-6} & {0} & {8} \\ {7} & {-8} & {0} \end{array}\right], B=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {1} & {1} \\ {1} & {0} & {2} \\ {1} & {2} & {0} \end{array}\right], C=\left[\begin{array}{c} {2} \\ {-2} \\ {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex}Calculate AC, BC and (A + B)C. Also, verify that (A + B)C = AC + BC
question_text: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {6} & {7} \\ {-6} & {0} & {8} \\ {7} & {-8} & {0} \end{array}\right], B=\left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {1} & {1} \\ {1} & {0} & {2} \\ {1} & {2} & {0} \end{array}\right], C=\left[\begin{array}{c} {2} \\ {-2} \\ {3} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Calculate AC, BC and (A + B)C. Also, verify that (A + B)C = AC + BC
explanation: We have, A + B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {6} & {7} \\ {-6} & {0} & {8} \\ {7} & {-8} & {0} \end{array}\right] +\left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {1} & {1} \\ {1} & {0} & {2} \\ {1} & {2} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {7} & {8} \\ {-5} & {0} & {10} \\ {8} & {-6} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} So (A + B) C = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {7} & {8} \\ {-5} & {0} & {10} \\ {8} & {-6} & {0} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} {2} \\ {-2} \\ {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} {0-14+24} \\ {-10+0+30} \\ {16+12+0} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} {10} \\ {20} \\ {28} \end{array}\right] ~...(i){/tex} Further AC = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {6} & {7} \\ {-6} & {0} & {8} \\ {7} & {-8} & {0} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} {2} \\ {-2} \\ {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} {0-12+21} \\ {-12+0+24} \\ {14+16+0} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} {9} \\ {12} \\ {30} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and BC = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {1} & {1} \\ {1} & {0} & {2} \\ {1} & {2} & {0} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} {2} \\ {-2} \\ {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} {0-2+3} \\ {2+0+6} \\ {2-4+0} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} {1} \\ {8} \\ {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} So AC + BC = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} {9} \\ {12} \\ {30} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{c} {1} \\ {8} \\ {-2} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} {10} \\ {20} \\ {28} \end{array}\right] ~...(ii){/tex} Clearly, from (i) and (ii), it follows that (A + B) C = AC + BC
solution: We have, A + B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {6} & {7} \\ {-6} & {0} & {8} \\ {7} & {-8} & {0} \end{array}\right] +\left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {1} & {1} \\ {1} & {0} & {2} \\ {1} & {2} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {7} & {8} \\ {-5} & {0} & {10} \\ {8} & {-6} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} So (A + B) C = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {7} & {8} \\ {-5} & {0} & {10} \\ {8} & {-6} & {0} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} {2} \\ {-2} \\ {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} {0-14+24} \\ {-10+0+30} \\ {16+12+0} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} {10} \\ {20} \\ {28} \end{array}\right] ~...(i){/tex} Further AC = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {6} & {7} \\ {-6} & {0} & {8} \\ {7} & {-8} & {0} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} {2} \\ {-2} \\ {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} {0-12+21} \\ {-12+0+24} \\ {14+16+0} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} {9} \\ {12} \\ {30} \end{array}\right]{/tex} and BC = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {1} & {1} \\ {1} & {0} & {2} \\ {1} & {2} & {0} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} {2} \\ {-2} \\ {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} {0-2+3} \\ {2+0+6} \\ {2-4+0} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} {1} \\ {8} \\ {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex} So AC + BC = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} {9} \\ {12} \\ {30} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{c} {1} \\ {8} \\ {-2} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} {10} \\ {20} \\ {28} \end{array}\right] ~...(ii){/tex} Clearly, from (i) and (ii), it follows that (A + B) C = AC + BC
solution_html:
We have, A + B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {6} & {7} \\ {-6} & {0} & {8} \\ {7} & {-8} & {0} \end{array}\right] +\left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {1} & {1} \\ {1} & {0} & {2} \\ {1} & {2} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {7} & {8} \\ {-5} & {0} & {10} \\ {8} & {-6} & {0} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
So (A + B) C = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {7} & {8} \\ {-5} & {0} & {10} \\ {8} & {-6} & {0} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} {2} \\ {-2} \\ {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} {0-14+24} \\ {-10+0+30} \\ {16+12+0} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} {10} \\ {20} \\ {28} \end{array}\right] ~...(i){/tex}
Further AC = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} {0} & {6} & {7} \\ {-6} & {0} & {8} \\ {7} & {-8} & {0} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{c} {2} \\ {-2} \\ {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} {0-12+21} \\ {-12+0+24} \\ {14+16+0} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} {9} \\ {12} \\ {30} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
and BC = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} {0} & {1} & {1} \\ {1} & {0} & {2} \\ {1} & {2} & {0} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l} {2} \\ {-2} \\ {3} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{l} {0-2+3} \\ {2+0+6} \\ {2-4+0} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} {1} \\ {8} \\ {-2} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
So AC + BC = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c} {9} \\ {12} \\ {30} \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{c} {1} \\ {8} \\ {-2} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{c} {10} \\ {20} \\ {28} \end{array}\right] ~...(ii){/tex}
Clearly, from (i) and (ii), it follows that
(A + B) C = AC + BC
