question_html: If {tex}A ^ { T } = \left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 3 } & { 4 } \\ { - 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { - 1 } & { 2 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 2 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}, then find AT - BT.
question_text: If {tex}A ^ { T } = \left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 3 } & { 4 } \\ { - 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { - 1 } & { 2 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 2 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} , then find A T - B T .
explanation: According to the question, {tex}A ^ { T } = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 3 } & { 4 } \\ { - 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { - 1 } & { 2 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 2 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} Transpose of {tex}B = {B^T} = \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&1 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex} [interchange the elements of rows and columns] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}A ^ { T } - B ^ { T } = \left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 3 } & { 4 } \\ { - 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] - \left[ \begin{array} { r r } { - 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 2 } & { 2 } \\ { 1 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 3 + 1 } & { 4 - 1 } \\ { - 1 - 2 } & { 2 - 2 } \\ { 0 - 1 } & { 1 - 3 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 4 } & { 3 } \\ { - 3 } & { 0 } \\ { - 1 } & { - 2 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
solution: According to the question, {tex}A ^ { T } = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 3 } & { 4 } \\ { - 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { - 1 } & { 2 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 2 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} Transpose of {tex}B = {B^T} = \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&1 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex} [interchange the elements of rows and columns] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}A ^ { T } - B ^ { T } = \left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 3 } & { 4 } \\ { - 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] - \left[ \begin{array} { r r } { - 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 2 } & { 2 } \\ { 1 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 3 + 1 } & { 4 - 1 } \\ { - 1 - 2 } & { 2 - 2 } \\ { 0 - 1 } & { 1 - 3 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 4 } & { 3 } \\ { - 3 } & { 0 } \\ { - 1 } & { - 2 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question,  {tex}A ^ { T } = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 3 } & { 4 } \\ { - 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} and {tex}B = \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { - 1 } & { 2 } & { 1 } \\ { 1 } & { 2 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
Transpose of {tex}B = {B^T} = \left[ \begin{gathered} \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1}&1 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 2&2 \end{array} \hfill \\ \begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&3 \end{array} \hfill \\ \end{gathered} \right]{/tex}[interchange the elements of rows and columns]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}  {tex}A ^ { T } - B ^ { T } = \left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 3 } & { 4 } \\ { - 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] - \left[ \begin{array} { r r } { - 1 } & { 1 } \\ { 2 } & { 2 } \\ { 1 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 3 + 1 } & { 4 - 1 } \\ { - 1 - 2 } & { 2 - 2 } \\ { 0 - 1 } & { 1 - 3 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 4 } & { 3 } \\ { - 3 } & { 0 } \\ { - 1 } & { - 2 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 2 x + 1 } & { 5 x } \\ { 0 } & { y ^ { 2 } + 1 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { x + 3 } & { 10 } \\ { 0 } & { 26 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}, find the value of (x + y)
question_text: If {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 2 x + 1 } & { 5 x } \\ { 0 } & { y ^ { 2 } + 1 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { x + 3 } & { 10 } \\ { 0 } & { 26 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} , find the value of (x + y)
explanation: Given that {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 2 x + 1 } & { 5 x } \\ { 0 } & { y ^ { 2 } + 1 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { x + 3 } & { 10 } \\ { 0 } & { 26 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} The corresponding entries of the equal matrices are equal. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x + 1 = x + 3, y 2 + 1 = 26, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x - x = 2, y 2 = 25 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2, y = {tex}\pm{/tex} 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2, y = 5 or x = 2, y = - 5 {tex}\therefore{/tex} x + y = 7 or - 3
solution: Given that {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 2 x + 1 } & { 5 x } \\ { 0 } & { y ^ { 2 } + 1 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { x + 3 } & { 10 } \\ { 0 } & { 26 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} The corresponding entries of the equal matrices are equal. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x + 1 = x + 3, y 2 + 1 = 26, {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x - x = 2, y 2 = 25 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2, y = {tex}\pm{/tex} 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2, y = 5 or x = 2, y = - 5 {tex}\therefore{/tex} x + y = 7 or - 3
solution_html:
Given that {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 2 x + 1 } & { 5 x } \\ { 0 } & { y ^ { 2 } + 1 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { x + 3 } & { 10 } \\ { 0 } & { 26 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
The corresponding entries of the equal matrices are equal.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x + 1 = x + 3, y2 + 1 = 26,
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x - x  = 2, y2 = 25
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2, y = {tex}\pm{/tex} 5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2, y = 5 or x = 2, y = - 5
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x + y = 7 or - 3

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{2} & {3} \\ {5} & {7}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{1} & {-3} \\ {-2} & {4}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{-4} & {6} \\ {-9} & {x}\end{array}\right]{/tex}, find x.
question_text: If {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{2} & {3} \\ {5} & {7}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{1} & {-3} \\ {-2} & {4}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{-4} & {6} \\ {-9} & {x}\end{array}\right]{/tex} , find x.
explanation: Given that {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2} & {3} \\ {5} & {7}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc}{1} & {-3} \\ {-2} & {4}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{-4} & {6} \\ {-9} & {x}\end{array}\right]{/tex} By multiplication of matrices, we have {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2 \times 1 + 3 \times ( - 2)}&{2 \times ( - 3) + 3 \times 4} \\ {5 \times 1 + 7 \times ( - 2)}&{5 \times ( - 3) + 7 \times 4} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{-4} & {6} \\ {-9} & {x}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{-4} & {6} \\ {-9} & {13}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{-4} & {6} \\ {-9} & {x}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 13
solution: Given that {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2} & {3} \\ {5} & {7}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc}{1} & {-3} \\ {-2} & {4}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{-4} & {6} \\ {-9} & {x}\end{array}\right]{/tex} By multiplication of matrices, we have {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2 \times 1 + 3 \times ( - 2)}&{2 \times ( - 3) + 3 \times 4} \\ {5 \times 1 + 7 \times ( - 2)}&{5 \times ( - 3) + 7 \times 4} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{-4} & {6} \\ {-9} & {x}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{-4} & {6} \\ {-9} & {13}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{-4} & {6} \\ {-9} & {x}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 13
solution_html:
Given that {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2} & {3} \\ {5} & {7}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc}{1} & {-3} \\ {-2} & {4}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{-4} & {6} \\ {-9} & {x}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
By multiplication of matrices, we have
{tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2 \times 1 + 3 \times ( - 2)}&{2 \times ( - 3) + 3 \times 4} \\ {5 \times 1 + 7 \times ( - 2)}&{5 \times ( - 3) + 7 \times 4} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{-4} & {6} \\ {-9} & {x}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{-4} & {6} \\ {-9} & {13}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{-4} & {6} \\ {-9} & {x}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 13

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}x\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} 2 \\ 3 \end{array}} \right] + y\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1} \\ 1 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {10} \\ 5 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, find the value of x.
question_text: If {tex}x\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} 2 \\ 3 \end{array}} \right] + y\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 1} \\ 1 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{l}} {10} \\ 5 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} , find the value of x.
explanation: According to the question, {tex}x \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 2 } \\ { 3 } \end{array} \right] + y \left[ \begin{array} { l } { - 1 } \\ { 1 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 10 } \\ { 5 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 2 x } \\ { 3 x } \end{array} \right] + \left[ \begin{array} { c } { - y } \\ { y } \end{array} \right] =\left[ \begin{array} { c } { 10 } \\ { 5 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 2 x - y } \\ { 3 x + y } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 10 } \\ { 5 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} Comparing the corresponding elements, {tex}2x - y = 10{/tex} ...(i) and {tex}3x + y = 5{/tex} ...(ii) Adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), {tex}5x = 15{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 3
solution: According to the question, {tex}x \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 2 } \\ { 3 } \end{array} \right] + y \left[ \begin{array} { l } { - 1 } \\ { 1 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 10 } \\ { 5 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 2 x } \\ { 3 x } \end{array} \right] + \left[ \begin{array} { c } { - y } \\ { y } \end{array} \right] =\left[ \begin{array} { c } { 10 } \\ { 5 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 2 x - y } \\ { 3 x + y } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 10 } \\ { 5 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} Comparing the corresponding elements, {tex}2x - y = 10{/tex} ...(i) and {tex}3x + y = 5{/tex} ...(ii) Adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), {tex}5x = 15{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 3
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex}x \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 2 } \\ { 3 } \end{array} \right] + y \left[ \begin{array} { l } { - 1 } \\ { 1 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 10 } \\ { 5 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 2 x } \\ { 3 x } \end{array} \right] + \left[ \begin{array} { c } { - y } \\ { y } \end{array} \right] =\left[ \begin{array} { c } { 10 } \\ { 5 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 2 x - y } \\ { 3 x + y } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 10 } \\ { 5 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
Comparing the corresponding elements, 
{tex}2x - y = 10{/tex} ...(i)
and {tex}3x + y = 5{/tex} ...(ii)
Adding Eqs. (i) and (ii), 
{tex}5x = 15{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x = 3

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If I is the identity matrix and A is a square matrix such that A2 = A, then what is the value of (I + A)2 - 3A?
question_text: If I is the identity matrix and A is a square matrix such that A 2 = A, then what is the value of (I + A) 2 - 3A?
explanation: We are given that, I 2 is the identity matrix. A is a square matrix such that A 2 = A. We need to find the value of (I + A) 2 – 3A. We must understand the nature of the identity matrix. An identity matrix is a square matrix in which all the elements of the principal diagonal are ones and all other elements are zeroes. Take, (I + A) 2 – 3A = (I) 2 + (A) 2 + 2(I)(A) – 3A [ {tex}\because{/tex} by using the algebraic identity,] (x + y) 2 = x 2 + y 2 + 2xy] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (I 2 + A) 2 – 3A = (I 2 )(I 2 ) + A 2 + 2(I 2 A) – 3A By property of matrix, (I 2 )(I 2 ) = I 2 I 2 A = A {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (I + A) 2 – 3A = I 2 + A 2 + 2A – 3A {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (I + A) 2 – 3A = I 2 + A + 2A – 3A [ {tex}\because{/tex} , given in question, A 2 = A] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (I + A) 2 – 3A = I + 3A – 3A {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (I + A) 2 – 3A = I + 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (I + A) 2 – 3A = I Thus, the value of (I + A) 2 – 3A = I 2
solution: We are given that, I 2 is the identity matrix. A is a square matrix such that A 2 = A. We need to find the value of (I + A) 2 – 3A. We must understand the nature of the identity matrix. An identity matrix is a square matrix in which all the elements of the principal diagonal are ones and all other elements are zeroes. Take, (I + A) 2 – 3A = (I) 2 + (A) 2 + 2(I)(A) – 3A [ {tex}\because{/tex} by using the algebraic identity,] (x + y) 2 = x 2 + y 2 + 2xy] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (I 2 + A) 2 – 3A = (I 2 )(I 2 ) + A 2 + 2(I 2 A) – 3A By property of matrix, (I 2 )(I 2 ) = I 2 I 2 A = A {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (I + A) 2 – 3A = I 2 + A 2 + 2A – 3A {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (I + A) 2 – 3A = I 2 + A + 2A – 3A [ {tex}\because{/tex} , given in question, A 2 = A] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (I + A) 2 – 3A = I + 3A – 3A {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (I + A) 2 – 3A = I + 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (I + A) 2 – 3A = I Thus, the value of (I + A) 2 – 3A = I 2
solution_html:
We are given that,
I2 is the identity matrix.
A is a square matrix such that A2 = A.
We need to find the value of (I + A)2 – 3A.
We must understand the nature of the identity matrix.
An identity matrix is a square matrix in which all the elements of the principal diagonal are ones and all other elements are zeroes.
Take,
(I + A)2 – 3A = (I)2+ (A)2 + 2(I)(A) – 3A
[{tex}\because{/tex}  by using the algebraic identity,]
(x + y)2 = x2 + y2 + 2xy]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (I2 + A)2 – 3A = (I2)(I2) + A2 + 2(I2A) – 3A
By property of matrix,
(I2)(I2) = I2
I2A = A
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (I + A)2 – 3A = I2 + A2 + 2A – 3A
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (I + A)2 – 3A = I2 + A + 2A – 3A [{tex}\because{/tex}, given in question, A2 = A]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (I + A)2 – 3A = I + 3A – 3A
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (I + A)2 – 3A = I + 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (I + A)2 – 3A = I
Thus, the value of (I + A)2 – 3A = I2

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If AT = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} 3 & 4 \\ -1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} -1 & 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, find AT - BT.
question_text: If A T = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} 3 & 4 \\ -1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} -1 & 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , find A T - B T .
explanation: Given, A T = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & 4 \\ -1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -1 & 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now B T = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} -1 & 1 \\ 2 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} A T - B T = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & 4 \\ -1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} -1 & 1 \\ 2 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} A T - B T = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3+1 & 4-1 \\ -1-2 & 2-2 \\ 0-1 & 1-3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} A T - B T = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 4 & 3 \\ -3 & 0 \\ -1 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution: Given, A T = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & 4 \\ -1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -1 & 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now B T = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} -1 & 1 \\ 2 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} A T - B T = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & 4 \\ -1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} -1 & 1 \\ 2 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} A T - B T = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3+1 & 4-1 \\ -1-2 & 2-2 \\ 0-1 & 1-3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} A T - B T = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 4 & 3 \\ -3 & 0 \\ -1 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Given,
AT = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & 4 \\ -1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} -1 & 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now
BT = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} -1 & 1 \\ 2 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
AT - BT = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & 4 \\ -1 & 2 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{cc} -1 & 1 \\ 2 & 2 \\ 1 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
AT - BT = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3+1 & 4-1 \\ -1-2 & 2-2 \\ 0-1 & 1-3 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
AT - BT = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 4 & 3 \\ -3 & 0 \\ -1 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find the value of x + y from the following equation: {tex}2\left[\begin{array}{cc} x & 5 \\ 7 & y-3 \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & -4 \\ 1 & 2 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 7 & 6 \\ 15 & 14 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_text: Find the value of x + y from the following equation: {tex}2\left[\begin{array}{cc} x & 5 \\ 7 & y-3 \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & -4 \\ 1 & 2 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 7 & 6 \\ 15 & 14 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
explanation: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 x & 10 \\ 14 & 2 y-6 \end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & -4 \\ 1 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 7 & 6 \\ 15 & 14 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Using basic matrix operation - {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 x+3 & 6 \\ 15 & 2 y-4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 7 & 6 \\ 15 & 14 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Using equality of matrices, we get - 2x + 3 = 7 & 2y – 4 = 14 2x = 4 & 2y = 18 x = 2 & y = 9 {tex}\therefore{/tex} x + y = 2 + 9 = 11 {tex}\therefore{/tex} The required value of x + y is 11
solution: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 x & 10 \\ 14 & 2 y-6 \end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & -4 \\ 1 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 7 & 6 \\ 15 & 14 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Using basic matrix operation - {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 x+3 & 6 \\ 15 & 2 y-4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 7 & 6 \\ 15 & 14 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Using equality of matrices, we get - 2x + 3 = 7 & 2y – 4 = 14 2x = 4 & 2y = 18 x = 2 & y = 9 {tex}\therefore{/tex} x + y = 2 + 9 = 11 {tex}\therefore{/tex} The required value of x + y is 11
solution_html:
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 x & 10 \\ 14 & 2 y-6 \end{array}\right]{/tex}+{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & -4 \\ 1 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 7 & 6 \\ 15 & 14 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Using basic matrix operation -
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 x+3 & 6 \\ 15 & 2 y-4 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 7 & 6 \\ 15 & 14 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Using equality of matrices, we get -
2x + 3 = 7 & 2y – 4 = 14
2x = 4 & 2y = 18
x = 2 & y = 9
{tex}\therefore{/tex} x + y = 2 + 9 = 11
{tex}\therefore{/tex}The required value of x + y is 11

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Write the order of product matrix {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l } { 1 } \\ { 2 } \\ { 3 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { 2 } & { 3 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} .
question_text: Write the order of product matrix {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l } { 1 } \\ { 2 } \\ { 3 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { 2 } & { 3 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} .
explanation: If a matrix A has order m {tex}\times{/tex} n and other matrix B has order n {tex}\times{/tex} z, then the matrix AB has order m {tex}\times{/tex} z. Given, {tex}A = \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 1 } \\ { 2 } \\ { 3 } \end{array} \right] {/tex} and {tex}B = [ 2 \quad 3 \quad 4 ]{/tex} Order of matrix {tex}A=3\times1{/tex} Order of matrix {tex}B=1\times3{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Order of product matrix {tex}AB=3\times3{/tex} .
solution: If a matrix A has order m {tex}\times{/tex} n and other matrix B has order n {tex}\times{/tex} z, then the matrix AB has order m {tex}\times{/tex} z. Given, {tex}A = \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 1 } \\ { 2 } \\ { 3 } \end{array} \right] {/tex} and {tex}B = [ 2 \quad 3 \quad 4 ]{/tex} Order of matrix {tex}A=3\times1{/tex} Order of matrix {tex}B=1\times3{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} Order of product matrix {tex}AB=3\times3{/tex} .
solution_html:
If a matrix A has order m {tex}\times{/tex} n and other matrix B has order n {tex}\times{/tex} z, then the matrix AB has order m {tex}\times{/tex} z.
Given,  {tex}A = \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 1 } \\ { 2 } \\ { 3 } \end{array} \right] {/tex} and {tex}B = [ 2 \quad 3 \quad 4 ]{/tex}
Order of matrix  {tex}A=3\times1{/tex}
Order of matrix  {tex}B=1\times3{/tex} 
{tex}\therefore{/tex} Order of product matrix {tex}AB=3\times3{/tex} .

question_html: If a matrix has 5 elements, then write all possible orders it can have.
question_text: If a matrix has 5 elements, then write all possible orders it can have.
explanation: If a matrix has order m {tex}\times{/tex} n, then the total number of elements in the matrix is mn According to the question, a matrix has 5 elements. {tex}\therefore{/tex} possible order of this matrix are 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 and 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 5.
solution: If a matrix has order m {tex}\times{/tex} n, then the total number of elements in the matrix is mn According to the question, a matrix has 5 elements. {tex}\therefore{/tex} possible order of this matrix are 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 and 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 5.
solution_html:
If a matrix has order m {tex}\times{/tex} n, then the total number of elements in the matrix is mn

According to the question, a matrix has 5 elements.

{tex}\therefore{/tex}  possible order of this matrix are 5 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 and 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 5.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&{x - y} \\ {2x + y}&7 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&1 \\ 8&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} then find the value of y.
question_text: If {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&{x - y} \\ {2x + y}&7 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&1 \\ 8&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} then find the value of y.
explanation: According to the question, {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&{x - y} \\ {2x + y}&7 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&1 \\ 8&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Equating the corresponding elements, {tex}\Rightarrow x = 3 \ and\ x - y = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y = x - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2{/tex}
solution: According to the question, {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&{x - y} \\ {2x + y}&7 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&1 \\ 8&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Equating the corresponding elements, {tex}\Rightarrow x = 3 \ and\ x - y = 1{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y = x - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question,

{tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} x&{x - y} \\ {2x + y}&7 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&1 \\ 8&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
Equating the corresponding elements, 
{tex}\Rightarrow x = 3 \ and\ x - y = 1{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow y = x - 1 = 3 - 1 = 2{/tex}

subject: Mathematics
question_html: For a 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix A = [aij] whose elements are given by aij = {tex}\frac{i}{j}{/tex}, write the value of a12.
question_text: For a 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix A = [a ij ] whose elements are given by aij = {tex}\frac{i}{j}{/tex} , write the value of a 12 .
explanation: A matrix of order 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2, A = [a ij ]. a ij = {tex}\frac{i}{j}{/tex} We need to find the value of a 12 . Compare a ij with a 12 . We get, i = 1 j = 2 Putting these values in a ij = {tex}\frac{i}{j}{/tex} , a 12 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Thus, the value of a 12 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} .
solution: A matrix of order 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2, A = [a ij ]. a ij = {tex}\frac{i}{j}{/tex} We need to find the value of a 12 . Compare a ij with a 12 . We get, i = 1 j = 2 Putting these values in a ij = {tex}\frac{i}{j}{/tex} , a 12 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} Thus, the value of a 12 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} .
solution_html:
A matrix of order 2 {tex}\times{/tex}2, A = [aij].
aij = {tex}\frac{i}{j}{/tex}
We need to find the value of a12.
Compare aij with a12.
We get,
i = 1
j = 2
Putting these values in aij = {tex}\frac{i}{j}{/tex},
a12 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}
Thus, the value of a12 = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}.

subject: Mathematics
question_html:
From the following matrix equation, find the value of x.
{tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c } { x + y } & { 4 } \\ { - 5 } & { 3y } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 3 } & { 4 } \\ { - 5 } & { 6 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
question_text: From the following matrix equation, find the value of x. {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c } { x + y } & { 4 } \\ { - 5 } & { 3y } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 3 } & { 4 } \\ { - 5 } & { 6 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
explanation: According to the question, {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c } { x + y } & { 4 } \\ { - 5 } & { 3y } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 3 } & { 4 } \\ { - 5 } & { 6 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} Equating the corresponding elements, {tex}x + y = 3{/tex} ...(i) and {tex} 3y = 6{/tex} ...(ii) From Eq. (ii), we get {tex}\Rightarrow y = 2{/tex} On substituting y = 2 in Eq. (i), we get x + 2 = 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1
solution: According to the question, {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c } { x + y } & { 4 } \\ { - 5 } & { 3y } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 3 } & { 4 } \\ { - 5 } & { 6 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} Equating the corresponding elements, {tex}x + y = 3{/tex} ...(i) and {tex} 3y = 6{/tex} ...(ii) From Eq. (ii), we get {tex}\Rightarrow y = 2{/tex} On substituting y = 2 in Eq. (i), we get x + 2 = 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1
solution_html:
According to the question, 
{tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c } { x + y } & { 4 } \\ { - 5 } & { 3y } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 3 } & { 4 } \\ { - 5 } & { 6 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
Equating the corresponding elements, 
{tex}x + y = 3{/tex} ...(i)
and {tex} 3y = 6{/tex} ...(ii)
From Eq. (ii), we get
{tex}\Rightarrow y = 2{/tex}
On substituting y = 2 in Eq. (i), we get
x + 2 = 3 

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1

subject: Mathematics
question_html: Find x from the matrix equation {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 1 } & { 3 } \\ { 4 } & { 5 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { l } { x } \\ { 2 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 5 } \\ { 6 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}.
question_text: Find x from the matrix equation {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 1 } & { 3 } \\ { 4 } & { 5 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { l } { x } \\ { 2 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 5 } \\ { 6 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} .
explanation: According to the question, {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 1 } & { 3 } \\ { 4 } & { 5 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { l } { x } \\ { 2 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 5 } \\ { 6 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ \begin{array} { c } { x + 6 } \\ { 4 x + 10 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 5 } \\ { 6 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} [multiplying row by column] Equating the corresponding elements, {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} x + 6 = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -1
solution: According to the question, {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 1 } & { 3 } \\ { 4 } & { 5 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { l } { x } \\ { 2 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 5 } \\ { 6 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ \begin{array} { c } { x + 6 } \\ { 4 x + 10 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 5 } \\ { 6 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} [multiplying row by column] Equating the corresponding elements, {tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} x + 6 = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -1
solution_html:
According to the question, 
{tex}\left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 1 } & { 3 } \\ { 4 } & { 5 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { l } { x } \\ { 2 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 5 } \\ { 6 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ \begin{array} { c } { x + 6 } \\ { 4 x + 10 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 5 } \\ { 6 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} [multiplying row by column]
Equating the corresponding elements, 
{tex}\Rightarrow {/tex} x + 6 = 5 

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -1

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} \cos \alpha & -\sin \alpha \\ \sin \alpha & \cos \alpha \end{array}\right]{/tex}is an identity matrix, then write the value of {tex}\alpha{/tex}.
question_text: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} \cos \alpha & -\sin \alpha \\ \sin \alpha & \cos \alpha \end{array}\right]{/tex} is an identity matrix, then write the value of {tex}\alpha{/tex} .
explanation: According to the question, {tex}A = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { \cos \alpha } & { - \sin \alpha } \\ { \sin \alpha } & { \cos \alpha } \end{array} \right]{/tex} For A to be an identity matrix, {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c } { cos \alpha } & { - \sin \alpha } \\ { \sin\alpha } & { \cos \alpha } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\left( {\because I = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]} \right){/tex} Equating the element a 11 of both matrices, we get {tex}\cos \alpha = 1 \Rightarrow \cos \alpha = \cos 0 ^ { \circ } \quad \left[ \because \cos 0 ^ { \circ } = 1 \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \alpha = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} for {tex}\alpha = 0{/tex} , A is an identity matrix. {tex}[ \because \sin 0 = 0 ]{/tex}
solution: According to the question, {tex}A = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { \cos \alpha } & { - \sin \alpha } \\ { \sin \alpha } & { \cos \alpha } \end{array} \right]{/tex} For A to be an identity matrix, {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c } { cos \alpha } & { - \sin \alpha } \\ { \sin\alpha } & { \cos \alpha } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\left( {\because I = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]} \right){/tex} Equating the element a 11 of both matrices, we get {tex}\cos \alpha = 1 \Rightarrow \cos \alpha = \cos 0 ^ { \circ } \quad \left[ \because \cos 0 ^ { \circ } = 1 \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad \alpha = 0{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} for {tex}\alpha = 0{/tex} , A is an identity matrix. {tex}[ \because \sin 0 = 0 ]{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex}A = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { \cos \alpha } & { - \sin \alpha } \\ { \sin \alpha } & { \cos \alpha } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
For A to be an identity matrix, 
{tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c } { cos \alpha } & { - \sin \alpha } \\ { \sin\alpha } & { \cos \alpha } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 1 } & { 0 } \\ { 0 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\left( {\because I = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]} \right){/tex}
Equating the element a11 of both matrices, we get
{tex}\cos \alpha = 1 \Rightarrow \cos \alpha = \cos 0 ^ { \circ } \quad \left[ \because \cos 0 ^ { \circ } = 1 \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad \alpha = 0{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex}  for {tex}\alpha = 0{/tex}, A is an identity matrix. {tex}[ \because \sin 0 = 0 ]{/tex}

question_html: If {tex} \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2x + y}&{3y} \\ 0&4 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 6&0 \\ 0&4 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}, then find the value of x.
question_text: If {tex} \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2x + y}&{3y} \\ 0&4 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 6&0 \\ 0&4 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} , then find the value of x.
explanation: According to the question , {tex} \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2x + y}&{3y} \\ 0&4 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 6&0 \\ 0&4 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Equating the corresponding elements, {tex}\Rightarrow 2x+y=6\ \ and\ \ 3y=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2x+0=6{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2x=6{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x=3{/tex}
solution: According to the question , {tex} \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2x + y}&{3y} \\ 0&4 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 6&0 \\ 0&4 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} Equating the corresponding elements, {tex}\Rightarrow 2x+y=6\ \ and\ \ 3y=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow y=0{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2x+0=6{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow 2x=6{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow x=3{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex} \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {2x + y}&{3y} \\ 0&4 \end{array}} \right] = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 6&0 \\ 0&4 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}

Equating the corresponding elements,

{tex}\Rightarrow 2x+y=6\ \ and\ \ 3y=0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow y=0{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow 2x+0=6{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow 2x=6{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow x=3{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}A = \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 3 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} , then find A + A'.
question_text: If {tex}A = \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 3 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} , then find A + A'.
explanation: According to the question, {tex}A = \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 3 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad A ^ { \prime } = \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 1 } & { 3 } \\ { 2 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} [interchange the elements of rows and columns] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}A + A ^ { \prime } = \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 3 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right] + \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 1 } & { 3 } \\ { 2 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 2 } & { 5 } \\ { 5 } & { 8 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
solution: According to the question, {tex}A = \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 3 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore \quad A ^ { \prime } = \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 1 } & { 3 } \\ { 2 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} [interchange the elements of rows and columns] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}A + A ^ { \prime } = \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 3 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right] + \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 1 } & { 3 } \\ { 2 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 2 } & { 5 } \\ { 5 } & { 8 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
According to the question, {tex}A = \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 3 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore \quad A ^ { \prime } = \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 1 } & { 3 } \\ { 2 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} [interchange the elements of rows and columns]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}  {tex}A + A ^ { \prime } = \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 3 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right] + \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 1 } & { 3 } \\ { 2 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 2 } & { 5 } \\ { 5 } & { 8 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 2 x + y } & { 3 y } \\ { 0 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 6 } & { 0 } \\ { 6 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}, then find x.
question_text: If {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 2 x + y } & { 3 y } \\ { 0 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 6 } & { 0 } \\ { 6 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} , then find x.
explanation: We have, {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 2 x + y } & { 3 y } \\ { 0 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 6 } & { 0 } \\ { 6 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right]'{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 2 x + y } & { 3 y } \\ { 0 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 6 } & { 6 } \\ { 0 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} If two matrices are equal, then their corresponding elements are equal. {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}2x + y = 6{/tex} ...(i) and {tex}3y = 6{/tex} ...(ii) From Eq. (ii), {tex}y = 2{/tex} Substituting {tex}y = 2 {/tex} in Eq. (i), {tex}\Rightarrow 2x + 2 = 6{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x + 1 = 3{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}x = 2{/tex}
solution: We have, {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 2 x + y } & { 3 y } \\ { 0 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 6 } & { 0 } \\ { 6 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right]'{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 2 x + y } & { 3 y } \\ { 0 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 6 } & { 6 } \\ { 0 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} If two matrices are equal, then their corresponding elements are equal. {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}2x + y = 6{/tex} ...(i) and {tex}3y = 6{/tex} ...(ii) From Eq. (ii), {tex}y = 2{/tex} Substituting {tex}y = 2 {/tex} in Eq. (i), {tex}\Rightarrow 2x + 2 = 6{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x + 1 = 3{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}x = 2{/tex}
solution_html:
We have, {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 2 x + y } & { 3 y } \\ { 0 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 6 } & { 0 } \\ { 6 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right]'{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { 2 x + y } & { 3 y } \\ { 0 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { l l } { 6 } & { 6 } \\ { 0 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
If two matrices are equal, then their corresponding elements are equal.
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}2x + y = 6{/tex} ...(i)
and {tex}3y = 6{/tex} ...(ii)
From Eq. (ii), 
{tex}y = 2{/tex}
Substituting {tex}y = 2 {/tex} in Eq. (i), 
{tex}\Rightarrow 2x + 2 = 6{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}x + 1 = 3{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} {tex}x = 2{/tex}

question_html: For the following matrices A and B, verify that [AB]' = B'A'; {tex} A = \left[ \begin{array} { r } { 1 } \\ { - 4 } \\ { 3 } \end{array} \right] , B = \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { - 1 } & { 2 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}.
question_text: For the following matrices A and B, verify that [AB]' = B'A'; {tex} A = \left[ \begin{array} { r } { 1 } \\ { - 4 } \\ { 3 } \end{array} \right] , B = \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { - 1 } & { 2 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} .
explanation: Here {tex}A B = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 1 } \\ { - 4 } \\ { 3 } \end{array} \right] _ { 3 \times 1 } \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { - 1 } & { 2 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] _ { 1 \times 3 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad A B = \left[ \begin{array} { r r r } { - 1 } & { 2 } & { 1 } \\ { 4 } & { - 8 } & { - 4 } \\ { - 3 } & { 6 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} [multiplying row by column] {tex}\quad {\text{ LHS }} = (AB)' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{r}} { - 1}&4&{ - 3} \\ 2&{ - 8}&6 \\ 1&{ - 4}&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ...(i) [interchanging the elements of rows and columns] Now, {tex}B ^ { \prime } = \left[ \begin{array} { r } { - 1 } \\ { 2 } \\ { 1 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} and {tex}A' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 4}&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}{/tex} Therefore,RHS {tex}= B' A ^ { \prime } = \left[ \begin{array} { r } { - 1 } \\ { 2 } \\ { 1 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { - 4 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ \begin{array} { r r r } { - 1 } & { 4 } & { - 3 } \\ { 2 } & { - 8 } & { 6 } \\ { 1 } & { - 4 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} ...(ii) [multiplying row by column] Therefore,From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get (AB)' = B'A' {tex}\therefore{/tex} LHS = RHS.
solution: Here {tex}A B = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 1 } \\ { - 4 } \\ { 3 } \end{array} \right] _ { 3 \times 1 } \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { - 1 } & { 2 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] _ { 1 \times 3 }{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow \quad A B = \left[ \begin{array} { r r r } { - 1 } & { 2 } & { 1 } \\ { 4 } & { - 8 } & { - 4 } \\ { - 3 } & { 6 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} [multiplying row by column] {tex}\quad {\text{ LHS }} = (AB)' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{r}} { - 1}&4&{ - 3} \\ 2&{ - 8}&6 \\ 1&{ - 4}&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ...(i) [interchanging the elements of rows and columns] Now, {tex}B ^ { \prime } = \left[ \begin{array} { r } { - 1 } \\ { 2 } \\ { 1 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} and {tex}A' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 4}&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}{/tex} Therefore,RHS {tex}= B' A ^ { \prime } = \left[ \begin{array} { r } { - 1 } \\ { 2 } \\ { 1 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { - 4 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ \begin{array} { r r r } { - 1 } & { 4 } & { - 3 } \\ { 2 } & { - 8 } & { 6 } \\ { 1 } & { - 4 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} ...(ii) [multiplying row by column] Therefore,From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get (AB)' = B'A' {tex}\therefore{/tex} LHS = RHS.
solution_html:
Here {tex}A B = \left[ \begin{array} { c } { 1 } \\ { - 4 } \\ { 3 } \end{array} \right] _ { 3 \times 1 } \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { - 1 } & { 2 } & { 1 } \end{array} \right] _ { 1 \times 3 }{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow \quad A B = \left[ \begin{array} { r r r } { - 1 } & { 2 } & { 1 } \\ { 4 } & { - 8 } & { - 4 } \\ { - 3 } & { 6 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} [multiplying row by column]
{tex}\quad {\text{ LHS }} = (AB)' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{r}} { - 1}&4&{ - 3} \\ 2&{ - 8}&6 \\ 1&{ - 4}&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} ...(i) [interchanging the elements of rows and columns]
Now, {tex}B ^ { \prime } = \left[ \begin{array} { r } { - 1 } \\ { 2 } \\ { 1 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
and {tex}A' = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&{ - 4}&3 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}{/tex} Therefore,RHS {tex}= B' A ^ { \prime } = \left[ \begin{array} { r } { - 1 } \\ { 2 } \\ { 1 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { c c c } { 1 } & { - 4 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}{tex} = \left[ \begin{array} { r r r } { - 1 } & { 4 } & { - 3 } \\ { 2 } & { - 8 } & { 6 } \\ { 1 } & { - 4 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} ...(ii) [multiplying row by column]
Therefore,From Eqs. (i) and (ii), we get (AB)' = B'A'
{tex}\therefore{/tex} LHS = RHS.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If A is a matrix of order 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 and B is a matrix of order 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 3, find the order of the matrix of AB.
question_text: If A is a matrix of order 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 and B is a matrix of order 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 3, find the order of the matrix of AB.
explanation: We are given that, Order of matrix A = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 Order of matrix B = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 We need to find the order of the matrix of AB. We know that, For matrices X and Y such that, Order of X = m {tex}\times{/tex} n Order of Y = r {tex}\times{/tex} s In order to multiply the two matrices X and Y, the number of columns in X must be equal to the number of rows in Y. That is, n = r And order of the resulting matrix, XY is given as Order of XY = m {tex}\times{/tex} s Provided n = r. So, we know Order of A = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 Then, Number of rows = 3 Number of columns = 4 Order of B = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 Then, Number of rows = 4 Number of columns = 3 Note that, Number of columns in A = Number of rows in B Thus, order of AB = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3
solution: We are given that, Order of matrix A = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 Order of matrix B = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 We need to find the order of the matrix of AB. We know that, For matrices X and Y such that, Order of X = m {tex}\times{/tex} n Order of Y = r {tex}\times{/tex} s In order to multiply the two matrices X and Y, the number of columns in X must be equal to the number of rows in Y. That is, n = r And order of the resulting matrix, XY is given as Order of XY = m {tex}\times{/tex} s Provided n = r. So, we know Order of A = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4 Then, Number of rows = 3 Number of columns = 4 Order of B = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 Then, Number of rows = 4 Number of columns = 3 Note that, Number of columns in A = Number of rows in B Thus, order of AB = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3
solution_html:
We are given that,
Order of matrix A = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4
Order of matrix B = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 3
We need to find the order of the matrix of AB.
We know that,
For matrices X and Y such that,
Order of X = m {tex}\times{/tex} n
Order of Y = r {tex}\times{/tex} s
In order to multiply the two matrices X and Y, the number of columns in X must be equal to the number of rows in Y. That is,
n = r
And order of the resulting matrix, XY is given as
Order of XY = m {tex}\times{/tex} s
Provided n = r.
So, we know
Order of A = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 4
Then,
Number of rows = 3
Number of columns = 4
Order of B = 4 {tex}\times{/tex} 3
Then,
Number of rows = 4
Number of columns = 3
Note that,
Number of columns in A = Number of rows in B
Thus, order of AB = 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 7 & 11 \\ k & 23 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, then write the value of k.
question_text: If {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 7 & 11 \\ k & 23 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , then write the value of k.
explanation: We are given, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 7 & 11 \\ k & 23 \end{array}\right]{/tex} We need to find the value of k. Take Left Hand Side (LHS) of the matrix equation. LHS = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} In the multiplication of matrices, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} b_{11} & b_{12} \\ b_{21} & b_{22} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} c_{11} & c_{12} \\ c_{21} & c_{22} \end{array}\right]{/tex} For c 11 : dot multiply the matching members of 1 st row of first matrix and 1 st column of second matrix and then sum up. (a 11 a 12 )(b 11 b 21 ) = a 11 {tex}\times{/tex} b 11 + a 12 {tex}\times{/tex} b 21 Thus, (1 2)(3 2) = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 2)(3 2) = 3 + 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 2)(3 2) = 7 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 7 & \end{array}\right]{/tex} For c 12 : dot multiply the matching members of 1 st row of first matrix and 2 nd column of second matrix and then sum up. (a 11 a 12 )(b 12 b 21 ) = a 11 {tex}\times{/tex} b 12 + a 12 {tex}\times{/tex} b 22 Thus, (1 2)(1 5) = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 2)(1 5) = 1 + 10 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 2)(1 5) = 11 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 7 & 11 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Similarly, do the same for other elements. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 7 & 11 \\ (3 \times 3)+(4 \times 2) & (3 \times 1)+(4 \times 5) \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 7 & 11 \\ 9+8 & 3+20 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 7 & 11 \\ 17 & 23 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Since, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 7 & 11 \\ k & 23 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Substituting the value of LHS, {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{cc} 7 & 11 \\ 17 & 23 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 7 & 11 \\ k & 23 \end{array}\right]{/tex} We know by the property of matrices, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} b_{11} & b_{12} \\ b_{21} & b_{22} \end{array}\right]{/tex} This implies, a 11 = b 11 , a 12 = b 12 , a 21 = b 21 and a 22 = b 22 17 = k Hence, k = 17.
solution: We are given, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 7 & 11 \\ k & 23 \end{array}\right]{/tex} We need to find the value of k. Take Left Hand Side (LHS) of the matrix equation. LHS = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} In the multiplication of matrices, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} b_{11} & b_{12} \\ b_{21} & b_{22} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} c_{11} & c_{12} \\ c_{21} & c_{22} \end{array}\right]{/tex} For c 11 : dot multiply the matching members of 1 st row of first matrix and 1 st column of second matrix and then sum up. (a 11 a 12 )(b 11 b 21 ) = a 11 {tex}\times{/tex} b 11 + a 12 {tex}\times{/tex} b 21 Thus, (1 2)(3 2) = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 2)(3 2) = 3 + 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 2)(3 2) = 7 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 7 & \end{array}\right]{/tex} For c 12 : dot multiply the matching members of 1 st row of first matrix and 2 nd column of second matrix and then sum up. (a 11 a 12 )(b 12 b 21 ) = a 11 {tex}\times{/tex} b 12 + a 12 {tex}\times{/tex} b 22 Thus, (1 2)(1 5) = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 2)(1 5) = 1 + 10 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 2)(1 5) = 11 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 7 & 11 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Similarly, do the same for other elements. {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 7 & 11 \\ (3 \times 3)+(4 \times 2) & (3 \times 1)+(4 \times 5) \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 7 & 11 \\ 9+8 & 3+20 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 7 & 11 \\ 17 & 23 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Since, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 7 & 11 \\ k & 23 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Substituting the value of LHS, {tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{cc} 7 & 11 \\ 17 & 23 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 7 & 11 \\ k & 23 \end{array}\right]{/tex} We know by the property of matrices, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} b_{11} & b_{12} \\ b_{21} & b_{22} \end{array}\right]{/tex} This implies, a 11 = b 11 , a 12 = b 12 , a 21 = b 21 and a 22 = b 22 17 = k Hence, k = 17.
solution_html:
We are given,
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 7 & 11 \\ k & 23 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
We need to find the value of k.
Take Left Hand Side (LHS) of the matrix equation.
LHS = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
In the multiplication of matrices,
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} b_{11} & b_{12} \\ b_{21} & b_{22} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} c_{11} & c_{12} \\ c_{21} & c_{22} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
For c11: dot multiply the matching members of 1st row of first matrix and 1st column of second matrix and then sum up.
(a11 a12)(b11 b21) = a11 {tex}\times{/tex} b11 + a12 {tex}\times{/tex} b21
Thus,
(1 2)(3 2) = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 2)(3 2) = 3 + 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 2)(3 2) = 7
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 7 & \end{array}\right]{/tex}
For c12: dot multiply the matching members of 1st row of first matrix and 2nd column of second matrix and then sum up.
(a11 a12)(b12 b21) = a11 {tex}\times{/tex} b12 + a12 {tex}\times{/tex} b22
Thus,
(1 2)(1 5) = 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 1 + 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 2)(1 5) = 1 + 10
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (1 2)(1 5) = 11
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 7 & 11 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Similarly, do the same for other elements.
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 7 & 11 \\ (3 \times 3)+(4 \times 2) & (3 \times 1)+(4 \times 5) \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 7 & 11 \\ 9+8 & 3+20 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex}{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 7 & 11 \\ 17 & 23 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Since,
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 1 & 2 \\ 3 & 4 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 7 & 11 \\ k & 23 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Substituting the value of LHS,
{tex}\Rightarrow\left[\begin{array}{cc} 7 & 11 \\ 17 & 23 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 7 & 11 \\ k & 23 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
We know by the property of matrices,
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} b_{11} & b_{12} \\ b_{21} & b_{22} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
This implies,
a11 = b11, a12 = b12, a21 = b21 and a22 = b22
17 = k
Hence, k = 17.

question_html: If matrix A = [1 2 3], then write AA'.
question_text: If matrix A = [1 2 3], then write AA'.
explanation: According to the question, A = [1 2 3] {tex}\therefore \quad A ^ { T } = \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 1 } \\ { 2 } \\ { 3 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} [interchange the elements of rows and columns] Now, {tex}A A^T = \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { 1 } & { 2 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 1 } \\ { 2 } \\ { 3 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}= [ (1 \times 1 ) + ( 2 \times 2 ) + ( 3 \times 3 ) ]{/tex} = [1 + 4 + 9] = [14]
solution: According to the question, A = [1 2 3] {tex}\therefore \quad A ^ { T } = \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 1 } \\ { 2 } \\ { 3 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} [interchange the elements of rows and columns] Now, {tex}A A^T = \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { 1 } & { 2 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 1 } \\ { 2 } \\ { 3 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}= [ (1 \times 1 ) + ( 2 \times 2 ) + ( 3 \times 3 ) ]{/tex} = [1 + 4 + 9] = [14]
solution_html:
According to the question, A = [1 2 3]
{tex}\therefore \quad A ^ { T } = \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 1 } \\ { 2 } \\ { 3 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} [interchange the elements of rows and columns]
Now, {tex}A A^T = \left[ \begin{array} { l l l } { 1 } & { 2 } & { 3 } \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array} { l } { 1 } \\ { 2 } \\ { 3 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex}= [ (1 \times 1 ) + ( 2 \times 2 ) + ( 3 \times 3 ) ]{/tex}
= [1 + 4 + 9] = [14]

question_html: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, CalculateAAT.
question_text: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , CalculateAA T .
explanation: Given: A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{array}\right]_{3 \times 1}{/tex} Now, firstly we find the A T {tex}A^T{/tex} = [1 2 3] 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 So, the product AA T will be A {tex}A^T{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} [1 2 3] = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 4 & 6 \\ 3 & 6 & 9 \end{array}\right]{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 which is the matrix {tex}AA^T{/tex} .
solution: Given: A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{array}\right]_{3 \times 1}{/tex} Now, firstly we find the A T {tex}A^T{/tex} = [1 2 3] 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 So, the product AA T will be A {tex}A^T{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} [1 2 3] = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 4 & 6 \\ 3 & 6 & 9 \end{array}\right]{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex} 3 which is the matrix {tex}AA^T{/tex} .
solution_html:
Given:
A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{array}\right]_{3 \times 1}{/tex}
Now, firstly we find the AT
{tex}A^T{/tex} = [1 2 3] 1 {tex}\times{/tex} 3
So, the product AAT will be
A{tex}A^T{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{l} 1 \\ 2 \\ 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex}[1 2 3]
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 2 & 3 \\ 2 & 4 & 6 \\ 3 & 6 & 9 \end{array}\right]{/tex} 3 {tex}\times{/tex}3 

which is the matrix  {tex}AA^T{/tex} .

question_html: Find the value of x from the following: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 x-y & 5 \\ 3 & y \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{rr} 6 & 5 \\ 3 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}.
question_text: Find the value of x from the following: {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 x-y & 5 \\ 3 & y \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{rr} 6 & 5 \\ 3 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} .
explanation: We are given with matrix equation, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 x-y & 5 \\ 3 & y \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 6 & 5 \\ 3 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} We need to find the values of x and y using the property of the equality of matrices. We know by the property of matrices, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} b_{11} & b_{12} \\ b_{21} & b_{22} \end{array}\right]{/tex} This implies, a 11 = b 11 , a 12 = b 12 , a 21 = b 21 and a 22 = b 22 So, if we have {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 x-y & 5 \\ 3 & y \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 6 & 5 \\ 3 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} By equating the corresponding elements of two matrices ,we get the following equations. 2x – y = 6 …(i) 5 = 5 3 = 3 y = -2 …(ii) To solve for x and y, we have equations (i) and (ii). From equation (ii), y = -2 Substituting y = -2 in equation (i), we get 2x – y = 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x – (-2) = 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x + 2 = 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 6 – 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{4}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2 Thus, we get x = 2 and y = -2.
solution: We are given with matrix equation, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 x-y & 5 \\ 3 & y \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 6 & 5 \\ 3 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} We need to find the values of x and y using the property of the equality of matrices. We know by the property of matrices, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} b_{11} & b_{12} \\ b_{21} & b_{22} \end{array}\right]{/tex} This implies, a 11 = b 11 , a 12 = b 12 , a 21 = b 21 and a 22 = b 22 So, if we have {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 x-y & 5 \\ 3 & y \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 6 & 5 \\ 3 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} By equating the corresponding elements of two matrices ,we get the following equations. 2x – y = 6 …(i) 5 = 5 3 = 3 y = -2 …(ii) To solve for x and y, we have equations (i) and (ii). From equation (ii), y = -2 Substituting y = -2 in equation (i), we get 2x – y = 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x – (-2) = 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x + 2 = 6 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 6 – 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{4}{2}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2 Thus, we get x = 2 and y = -2.
solution_html:
We are given with matrix equation,
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 x-y & 5 \\ 3 & y \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 6 & 5 \\ 3 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
We need to find the values of x and y using the property of the equality of matrices.
We know by the property of matrices,
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} b_{11} & b_{12} \\ b_{21} & b_{22} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
This implies,
a11 = b11, a12 = b12, a21 = b21 and a22 = b22
So, if we have
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 2 x-y & 5 \\ 3 & y \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 6 & 5 \\ 3 & -2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
By equating the corresponding elements of two matrices ,we get the following equations.
2x – y = 6 …(i)
5 = 5
3 = 3
y = -2 …(ii)
To solve for x and y, we have equations (i) and (ii).
From equation (ii),
y = -2
Substituting y = -2 in equation (i), we get
2x – y = 6
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x – (-2) = 6
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x + 2 = 6
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 6 – 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 2x = 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{4}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2
Thus, we get x = 2 and y = -2.

question_html: Find the value of y, if {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} x-y & 2 \\ x & 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 2 \\ 3 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}.
question_text: Find the value of y, if {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} x-y & 2 \\ x & 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 2 \\ 3 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} .
explanation: We are given that, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} x-y & 2 \\ x & 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 2 \\ 3 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} We need to find the values of x and y. We know by the property of matrices, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} b_{11} & b_{12} \\ b_{21} & b_{22} \end{array}\right]{/tex} This implies, a 11 = b 11 , a 12 = b 12 , a 21 = b 21 and a 22 = b 22 So, if we have {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} x-y & 2 \\ x & 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 2 \\ 3 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Corresponding elements of two matrices are equal. That is, x – y = 2 …(i) 2 = 2 x = 3 From equation (ii), x = 3 Substituting x = 3 in equation (i), we get 3 – y = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 3 – 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 1 Thus, we get x = 3 and y = 1.
solution: We are given that, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} x-y & 2 \\ x & 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 2 \\ 3 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} We need to find the values of x and y. We know by the property of matrices, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} b_{11} & b_{12} \\ b_{21} & b_{22} \end{array}\right]{/tex} This implies, a 11 = b 11 , a 12 = b 12 , a 21 = b 21 and a 22 = b 22 So, if we have {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} x-y & 2 \\ x & 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 2 \\ 3 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Corresponding elements of two matrices are equal. That is, x – y = 2 …(i) 2 = 2 x = 3 From equation (ii), x = 3 Substituting x = 3 in equation (i), we get 3 – y = 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 3 – 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 1 Thus, we get x = 3 and y = 1.
solution_html:
We are given that,
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} x-y & 2 \\ x & 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 2 \\ 3 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
We need to find the values of x and y.
We know by the property of matrices,
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} b_{11} & b_{12} \\ b_{21} & b_{22} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
This implies,
a11 = b11, a12 = b12, a21 = b21 and a22 = b22
So, if we have
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} x-y & 2 \\ x & 5 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} 2 & 2 \\ 3 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Corresponding elements of two matrices are equal.
That is,
x – y = 2 …(i)
2 = 2
x = 3 
From equation (ii),
x = 3
Substituting x = 3 in equation (i), we get
3 – y = 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 3 – 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 1
Thus, we get x = 3 and y = 1.

question_html: Find the value of x, if {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} 3 x+y & -y \\ 2 y-x & 3 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{rr} 1 & 2 \\ -5 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
question_text: Find the value of x, if {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} 3 x+y & -y \\ 2 y-x & 3 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{rr} 1 & 2 \\ -5 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
explanation: We are given that, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 x+y & -y \\ 2 y-x & 3 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 \\ -5 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} We need to find the values of x and y. We know by the property of matrices, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} b_{11} & b_{12} \\ b_{21} & b_{22} \end{array}\right]{/tex} This implies, a 11 = b 11 , a 12 = b 12 , a 21 = b 21 and a 22 = b 22 So, if we have {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 x+y & -y \\ 2 y-x & 3 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 \\ -5 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Corresponding elements of two matrices are equal. That is, 3x + y = 1 …(i) -y = 2 …(ii) 2y – x = -5 …(iii) From equation (ii), -y = 2 Multiplying both sides by -1, -1 × -y = -1 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = -2 Substituting y = -2 3x + y = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x + (-2) = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x – 2 = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x = 1 + 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x = 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{3}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1 Thus, we get x = 1 and y = -2
solution: We are given that, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 x+y & -y \\ 2 y-x & 3 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 \\ -5 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} We need to find the values of x and y. We know by the property of matrices, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} b_{11} & b_{12} \\ b_{21} & b_{22} \end{array}\right]{/tex} This implies, a 11 = b 11 , a 12 = b 12 , a 21 = b 21 and a 22 = b 22 So, if we have {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 x+y & -y \\ 2 y-x & 3 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 \\ -5 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Corresponding elements of two matrices are equal. That is, 3x + y = 1 …(i) -y = 2 …(ii) 2y – x = -5 …(iii) From equation (ii), -y = 2 Multiplying both sides by -1, -1 × -y = -1 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = -2 Substituting y = -2 3x + y = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x + (-2) = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x – 2 = 1 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x = 1 + 2 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x = 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{3}{3}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1 Thus, we get x = 1 and y = -2
solution_html:
We are given that,
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 x+y & -y \\ 2 y-x & 3 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 \\ -5 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
We need to find the values of x and y.
We know by the property of matrices,
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} b_{11} & b_{12} \\ b_{21} & b_{22} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
This implies,
a11 = b11, a12 = b12, a21 = b21 and a22 = b22
So, if we have
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} 3 x+y & -y \\ 2 y-x & 3 \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 1 & 2 \\ -5 & 3 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Corresponding elements of two matrices are equal.
That is,
3x + y = 1 …(i)
-y = 2 …(ii)
2y – x = -5 …(iii)

From equation (ii),
-y = 2
Multiplying both sides by -1,
-1 × -y = -1 {tex}\times{/tex} 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = -2
Substituting y = -2 
3x + y = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x + (-2) = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x – 2 = 1
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x = 1 + 2
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 3x = 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = {tex}\frac{3}{3}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 1
Thus, we get x = 1 and y = -2

question_html: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}then prove that A2 - 4A - 5l = 0.
question_text: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} then prove that A 2 - 4A - 5l = 0.
explanation: Given: A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} To prove A 2 – 4A – 5 I 2 = O 2 Here I 2 is the identity matrix of order 2 and O 2 is the zero matrix of order 2. Now, we will find the matrix for A 2 , we get {tex}A^{2}=A \times A=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A 2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 \times 1+2 \times 2+2 \times 2 & 1 \times 2+2 \times 1+2 \times 2 & 1 \times 2+2 \times 2+2 \times 1 \\ 2 \times 1+1 \times 2+2 \times 2 & 2 \times 2+1 \times 1+2 \times 2 & 2 \times 2+1 \times 2+2 \times 1 \\ 2 \times 1+2 \times 2+1 \times 2 & 2 \times 2+2 \times 1+1 \times 2 & 2 \times 2+2 \times 2+1 \times 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} [as c ij = a i1 b 1j + a i2 b 2j + … + a in b nj ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A 2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1+4+4 & 2+2+4 & 2+4+2 \\ 2+2+4 & 4+1+4 & 4+2+2 \\ 2+4+2 & 4+2+2 & 4+4+1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A 2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 9 & 8 & 8 \\ 8 & 9 & 8 \\ 8 & 8 & 9 \end{array}\right]{/tex} .........(1) So, Substitute corresponding values from eqn(i) in equation A 2 – 4A – 5 I 2 , we get {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 9 & 8 & 8 \\ 8 & 9 & 8 \\ 8 & 8 & 9 \end{array}\right]-4\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]-5\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 9 & 8 & 8 \\ 8 & 9 & 8 \\ 8 & 8 & 9 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 \times 1 & 4 \times 2 & 4 \times 2 \\ 4 \times 2 & 4 \times 1 & 4 \times 2 \\ 4 \times 2 & 4 \times 2 & 4 \times 1 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 9 & 8 & 8 \\ 8 & 9 & 8 \\ 8 & 8 & 9 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{lll} 4 & 8 & 8 \\ 8 & 4 & 8 \\ 8 & 8 & 4 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{lll} 5 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 9-4-5 & 8-8-0 & 8-8-0 \\ 8-8-0 & 9-4-5 & 8-8-0 \\ 8-8-0 & 8-8-0 & 9-4-5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} [as r ij = a ij + b ij + c ij ], {tex}\Rightarrow=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = O 2 Hence the A 2 – 4A – 5I = O 2 (Proved)
solution: Given: A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} To prove A 2 – 4A – 5 I 2 = O 2 Here I 2 is the identity matrix of order 2 and O 2 is the zero matrix of order 2. Now, we will find the matrix for A 2 , we get {tex}A^{2}=A \times A=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A 2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 \times 1+2 \times 2+2 \times 2 & 1 \times 2+2 \times 1+2 \times 2 & 1 \times 2+2 \times 2+2 \times 1 \\ 2 \times 1+1 \times 2+2 \times 2 & 2 \times 2+1 \times 1+2 \times 2 & 2 \times 2+1 \times 2+2 \times 1 \\ 2 \times 1+2 \times 2+1 \times 2 & 2 \times 2+2 \times 1+1 \times 2 & 2 \times 2+2 \times 2+1 \times 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} [as c ij = a i1 b 1j + a i2 b 2j + … + a in b nj ] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A 2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1+4+4 & 2+2+4 & 2+4+2 \\ 2+2+4 & 4+1+4 & 4+2+2 \\ 2+4+2 & 4+2+2 & 4+4+1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A 2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 9 & 8 & 8 \\ 8 & 9 & 8 \\ 8 & 8 & 9 \end{array}\right]{/tex} .........(1) So, Substitute corresponding values from eqn(i) in equation A 2 – 4A – 5 I 2 , we get {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 9 & 8 & 8 \\ 8 & 9 & 8 \\ 8 & 8 & 9 \end{array}\right]-4\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]-5\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 9 & 8 & 8 \\ 8 & 9 & 8 \\ 8 & 8 & 9 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 \times 1 & 4 \times 2 & 4 \times 2 \\ 4 \times 2 & 4 \times 1 & 4 \times 2 \\ 4 \times 2 & 4 \times 2 & 4 \times 1 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 9 & 8 & 8 \\ 8 & 9 & 8 \\ 8 & 8 & 9 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{lll} 4 & 8 & 8 \\ 8 & 4 & 8 \\ 8 & 8 & 4 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{lll} 5 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 9-4-5 & 8-8-0 & 8-8-0 \\ 8-8-0 & 9-4-5 & 8-8-0 \\ 8-8-0 & 8-8-0 & 9-4-5 \end{array}\right]{/tex} [as r ij = a ij + b ij + c ij ], {tex}\Rightarrow=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = O 2 Hence the A 2 – 4A – 5I = O 2 (Proved)
solution_html:
Given: A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
To prove A2 – 4A – 5 I2 = O2
Here I2 is the identity matrix  of order 2 and O2 is the zero matrix of order 2.
Now, we will find the matrix for A2, we get
{tex}A^{2}=A \times A=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A2
={tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1 \times 1+2 \times 2+2 \times 2 & 1 \times 2+2 \times 1+2 \times 2 & 1 \times 2+2 \times 2+2 \times 1 \\ 2 \times 1+1 \times 2+2 \times 2 & 2 \times 2+1 \times 1+2 \times 2 & 2 \times 2+1 \times 2+2 \times 1 \\ 2 \times 1+2 \times 2+1 \times 2 & 2 \times 2+2 \times 1+1 \times 2 & 2 \times 2+2 \times 2+1 \times 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
[as cij = ai1b1j + ai2b2j + … + ainbnj]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 1+4+4 & 2+2+4 & 2+4+2 \\ 2+2+4 & 4+1+4 & 4+2+2 \\ 2+4+2 & 4+2+2 & 4+4+1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}

{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} A2 ={tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 9 & 8 & 8 \\ 8 & 9 & 8 \\ 8 & 8 & 9 \end{array}\right]{/tex} .........(1)
So, Substitute corresponding values from eqn(i) in equation
A2 – 4A – 5 I2, we get
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 9 & 8 & 8 \\ 8 & 9 & 8 \\ 8 & 8 & 9 \end{array}\right]-4\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 2 & 2 \\ 2 & 1 & 2 \\ 2 & 2 & 1 \end{array}\right]-5\left[\begin{array}{lll} 1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 9 & 8 & 8 \\ 8 & 9 & 8 \\ 8 & 8 & 9 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 \times 1 & 4 \times 2 & 4 \times 2 \\ 4 \times 2 & 4 \times 1 & 4 \times 2 \\ 4 \times 2 & 4 \times 2 & 4 \times 1 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 5 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 9 & 8 & 8 \\ 8 & 9 & 8 \\ 8 & 8 & 9 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{lll} 4 & 8 & 8 \\ 8 & 4 & 8 \\ 8 & 8 & 4 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{lll} 5 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 5 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 9-4-5 & 8-8-0 & 8-8-0 \\ 8-8-0 & 9-4-5 & 8-8-0 \\ 8-8-0 & 8-8-0 & 9-4-5 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
[as rij = aij + bij + cij],
{tex}\Rightarrow=\left[\begin{array}{lll} 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = O2
Hence the A2 – 4A – 5I = O2 (Proved)
