question_html: Express the matrix A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} 4 & 2 & -1 \\ 3 & 5 & 7 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}as the sum of a symmetric and a skew-symmetric matrix.
question_text: Express the matrix A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr} 4 & 2 & -1 \\ 3 & 5 & 7 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} as the sum of a symmetric and a skew-symmetric matrix.
explanation: Given, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & 2 & -1 \\ 3 & 5 & 7 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Then A T = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 & -2 \\ -1 & 7 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} X = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (A + A T ) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & 2 & -1 \\ 3 & 5 & 7 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 & -2 \\ -1 & 7 & 1 \end{array}\right]\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4+4 & 2+3 & -1+1 \\ 3+2 & 5+5 & 7-2 \\ 1-1 & -2+7 & 1+1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 8 & 5 & 0 \\ 5 & 10 & 5 \\ 0 & 5 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} X = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & \frac{5}{2} & 0 \\ \frac{5}{2} & 5 & \frac{5}{2} \\ 0 & \frac{5}{2} & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} X T = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & \frac{5}{2} & 0 \\ 5 & & 5 \\ \frac{5}{2} & 5 & \frac{5}{2} \\ 0 & \frac{5}{2} & 1 \end{array}\right]^{\mathrm{T}}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & \frac{5}{2} & 0 \\ 5 & & 5 \\ \frac{5}{2} & 5 & \frac{5}{2} \\ 0 & \frac{5}{2} & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = X {tex}\therefore{/tex} X is a symmetric matrix. Y = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (A - A T ) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & 2 & -1 \\ 3 & 5 & 7 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 & -2 \\ -1 & 7 & 1 \end{array}\right]\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4-4 & 2-3 & -1-1 \\ 3-2 & 5-5 & 7+2 \\ 1+1 & -2-7 & 1-1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & -1 & -2 \\ 1 & 0 & 9 \\ 2 & -9 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Y = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & -\frac{1}{2} & -1 \\ \frac{1}{2} & 0 & \frac{9}{2} \\ 1 & -\frac{9}{2} & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Y T = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & -\frac{1}{2} & -1 \\ \frac{1}{2} & 0 & \frac{9}{2} \\ 1 & -\frac{9}{2} & 0 \end{array}\right]^{\mathrm{T}}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & -\frac{1}{2} & -1 \\ \frac{1}{2} & 0 & \frac{9}{2} \\ 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & -\frac{9}{2} & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = Y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Y is a skew-symmetric matrix. Now, X + Y = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & \frac{5}{2} & 0 \\ \frac{5}{2} & 5 & \frac{5}{2} \\ 0 & \frac{5}{2} & 1 \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & -\frac{1}{2} & -1 \\ \frac{1}{2} & 0 & \frac{9}{2} \\ 1 & -\frac{9}{2} & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 4+0 & \frac{5}{2}-\frac{1}{2} & 0-1 \\ \frac{5}{2}+\frac{1}{2} & 5+0 & \frac{5}{2}+\frac{9}{2} \\ 0+1 & \frac{5}{2}-\frac{9}{2} & 1+0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & 2 & -1 \\ 3 & 5 & 7 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = A Hence, X + Y = A. Thus matrix A, is expressed as sum of symmetric and skew symmetric matrices.
solution: Given, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & 2 & -1 \\ 3 & 5 & 7 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Then A T = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 & -2 \\ -1 & 7 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} X = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (A + A T ) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & 2 & -1 \\ 3 & 5 & 7 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 & -2 \\ -1 & 7 & 1 \end{array}\right]\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4+4 & 2+3 & -1+1 \\ 3+2 & 5+5 & 7-2 \\ 1-1 & -2+7 & 1+1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 8 & 5 & 0 \\ 5 & 10 & 5 \\ 0 & 5 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex} X = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & \frac{5}{2} & 0 \\ \frac{5}{2} & 5 & \frac{5}{2} \\ 0 & \frac{5}{2} & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} X T = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & \frac{5}{2} & 0 \\ 5 & & 5 \\ \frac{5}{2} & 5 & \frac{5}{2} \\ 0 & \frac{5}{2} & 1 \end{array}\right]^{\mathrm{T}}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & \frac{5}{2} & 0 \\ 5 & & 5 \\ \frac{5}{2} & 5 & \frac{5}{2} \\ 0 & \frac{5}{2} & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = X {tex}\therefore{/tex} X is a symmetric matrix. Y = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (A - A T ) = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & 2 & -1 \\ 3 & 5 & 7 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 & -2 \\ -1 & 7 & 1 \end{array}\right]\right){/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4-4 & 2-3 & -1-1 \\ 3-2 & 5-5 & 7+2 \\ 1+1 & -2-7 & 1-1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & -1 & -2 \\ 1 & 0 & 9 \\ 2 & -9 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Y = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & -\frac{1}{2} & -1 \\ \frac{1}{2} & 0 & \frac{9}{2} \\ 1 & -\frac{9}{2} & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Y T = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & -\frac{1}{2} & -1 \\ \frac{1}{2} & 0 & \frac{9}{2} \\ 1 & -\frac{9}{2} & 0 \end{array}\right]^{\mathrm{T}}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & -\frac{1}{2} & -1 \\ \frac{1}{2} & 0 & \frac{9}{2} \\ 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & -\frac{9}{2} & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = Y {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Y is a skew-symmetric matrix. Now, X + Y = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & \frac{5}{2} & 0 \\ \frac{5}{2} & 5 & \frac{5}{2} \\ 0 & \frac{5}{2} & 1 \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & -\frac{1}{2} & -1 \\ \frac{1}{2} & 0 & \frac{9}{2} \\ 1 & -\frac{9}{2} & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 4+0 & \frac{5}{2}-\frac{1}{2} & 0-1 \\ \frac{5}{2}+\frac{1}{2} & 5+0 & \frac{5}{2}+\frac{9}{2} \\ 0+1 & \frac{5}{2}-\frac{9}{2} & 1+0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & 2 & -1 \\ 3 & 5 & 7 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = A Hence, X + Y = A. Thus matrix A, is expressed as sum of symmetric and skew symmetric matrices.
solution_html:
Given, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & 2 & -1 \\ 3 & 5 & 7 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Then AT = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 & -2 \\ -1 & 7 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
X = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex}(A + AT)
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & 2 & -1 \\ 3 & 5 & 7 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 & -2 \\ -1 & 7 & 1 \end{array}\right]\right){/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4+4 & 2+3 & -1+1 \\ 3+2 & 5+5 & 7-2 \\ 1-1 & -2+7 & 1+1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 8 & 5 & 0 \\ 5 & 10 & 5 \\ 0 & 5 & 2 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
X = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & \frac{5}{2} & 0 \\ \frac{5}{2} & 5 & \frac{5}{2} \\ 0 & \frac{5}{2} & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
XT = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & \frac{5}{2} & 0 \\ 5 & & 5 \\ \frac{5}{2} & 5 & \frac{5}{2} \\ 0 & \frac{5}{2} & 1 \end{array}\right]^{\mathrm{T}}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & \frac{5}{2} & 0 \\ 5 & & 5 \\ \frac{5}{2} & 5 & \frac{5}{2} \\ 0 & \frac{5}{2} & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}= X
{tex}\therefore{/tex} X is a symmetric matrix.
Y = {tex}\frac{1}{2}{/tex} (A - AT)
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left(\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & 2 & -1 \\ 3 & 5 & 7 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]-\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & 3 & 1 \\ 2 & 5 & -2 \\ -1 & 7 & 1 \end{array}\right]\right){/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4-4 & 2-3 & -1-1 \\ 3-2 & 5-5 & 7+2 \\ 1+1 & -2-7 & 1-1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\frac{1}{2}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & -1 & -2 \\ 1 & 0 & 9 \\ 2 & -9 & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Y = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & -\frac{1}{2} & -1 \\ \frac{1}{2} & 0 & \frac{9}{2} \\ 1 & -\frac{9}{2} & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
YT = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & -\frac{1}{2} & -1 \\ \frac{1}{2} & 0 & \frac{9}{2} \\ 1 & -\frac{9}{2} & 0 \end{array}\right]^{\mathrm{T}}=\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & -\frac{1}{2} & -1 \\ \frac{1}{2} & 0 & \frac{9}{2} \\ 2 & 0 & 0 \\ 1 & -\frac{9}{2} & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex} = Y
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} Y is a skew-symmetric matrix.
Now,
X + Y = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & \frac{5}{2} & 0 \\ \frac{5}{2} & 5 & \frac{5}{2} \\ 0 & \frac{5}{2} & 1 \end{array}\right]+\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 0 & -\frac{1}{2} & -1 \\ \frac{1}{2} & 0 & \frac{9}{2} \\ 1 & -\frac{9}{2} & 0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{lll} 4+0 & \frac{5}{2}-\frac{1}{2} & 0-1 \\ \frac{5}{2}+\frac{1}{2} & 5+0 & \frac{5}{2}+\frac{9}{2} \\ 0+1 & \frac{5}{2}-\frac{9}{2} & 1+0 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc} 4 & 2 & -1 \\ 3 & 5 & 7 \\ 1 & -2 & 1 \end{array}\right]{/tex}= A
Hence, X + Y = A.

Thus matrix A, is expressed as sum of symmetric and skew symmetric matrices.

question_html: If A = [aij] is a 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix such that aij = i + 2j, write A.
question_text: If A = [a ij ] is a 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix such that a ij = i + 2j, write A.
explanation: Given: A = [a ij ] is a 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix {tex}\therefore{/tex} A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array}\right]{/tex} ….......(i) Given that a ij = i + 2j So, a 11 = 1 + 2(1)= 1 + 2 = 3 a 12 = 1 + 2(2 )= 1 + 4 = 5 a 21 = 2 + 2(1 )= 2 + 2 = 4 a 22 = 2 + 2(2 )= 2 + 4 = 6 Putting the values in eq. (i), we get {tex}\therefore{/tex} A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 5 \\ 4 & 6 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution: Given: A = [a ij ] is a 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix {tex}\therefore{/tex} A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array}\right]{/tex} ….......(i) Given that a ij = i + 2j So, a 11 = 1 + 2(1)= 1 + 2 = 3 a 12 = 1 + 2(2 )= 1 + 4 = 5 a 21 = 2 + 2(1 )= 2 + 2 = 4 a 22 = 2 + 2(2 )= 2 + 4 = 6 Putting the values in eq. (i), we get {tex}\therefore{/tex} A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 5 \\ 4 & 6 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Given: A = [aij] is a 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix
{tex}\therefore{/tex} A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array}\right]{/tex} ….......(i)
Given that aij = i + 2j
So, a11 = 1 + 2(1)= 1 + 2 = 3
a12 = 1 + 2(2 )= 1 + 4 = 5
a21 = 2 + 2(1 )= 2 + 2 = 4
a22 = 2 + 2(2 )= 2 + 4 = 6
Putting the values in eq. (i), we get
{tex}\therefore{/tex} A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} 3 & 5 \\ 4 & 6 \end{array}\right]{/tex}

question_html: If {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} x+3 & 4 \\ y-4 & x+y \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{rr} 5 & 4 \\ 3 & 9 \end{array}\right]{/tex}, find x and y.
question_text: If {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr} x+3 & 4 \\ y-4 & x+y \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{rr} 5 & 4 \\ 3 & 9 \end{array}\right]{/tex} , find x and y.
explanation: We are given with, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} x+3 & 4 \\ y-4 & x+y \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 & 4 \\ 3 & 9 \end{array}\right]{/tex} We need to find the values of x and y. We know by the property of matrices, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} b_{11} & b_{12} \\ b_{21} & b_{22} \end{array}\right]{/tex} So, if we have {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} x+3 & 4 \\ y-4 & x+y \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 & 4 \\ 3 & 9 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Corresponding elements of two matrices are equal. That is, x + 3 = 5 …(i) y – 4 = 3 …(ii) x + y = 9 …(iii) To solve for x and y, we have three equations (i), (ii) and (iii). From equation (i), x + 3 = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 5 – 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2 From equation (ii), y – 4 = 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 3 + 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 7 We need not solve equation (iii) as we have got the values of x and y. Thus, the values of x = 2 and y = 7.
solution: We are given with, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} x+3 & 4 \\ y-4 & x+y \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 & 4 \\ 3 & 9 \end{array}\right]{/tex} We need to find the values of x and y. We know by the property of matrices, {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} b_{11} & b_{12} \\ b_{21} & b_{22} \end{array}\right]{/tex} So, if we have {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} x+3 & 4 \\ y-4 & x+y \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 & 4 \\ 3 & 9 \end{array}\right]{/tex} Corresponding elements of two matrices are equal. That is, x + 3 = 5 …(i) y – 4 = 3 …(ii) x + y = 9 …(iii) To solve for x and y, we have three equations (i), (ii) and (iii). From equation (i), x + 3 = 5 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 5 – 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2 From equation (ii), y – 4 = 3 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 3 + 4 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 7 We need not solve equation (iii) as we have got the values of x and y. Thus, the values of x = 2 and y = 7.
solution_html:
We are given with,
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} x+3 & 4 \\ y-4 & x+y \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 & 4 \\ 3 & 9 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
We need to find the values of x and y.
We know by the property of matrices,
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{ll} b_{11} & b_{12} \\ b_{21} & b_{22} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
So, if we have
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc} x+3 & 4 \\ y-4 & x+y \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} 5 & 4 \\ 3 & 9 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Corresponding elements of two matrices are equal.
That is,
x + 3 = 5 …(i)
y – 4 = 3 …(ii)
x + y = 9 …(iii)
To solve for x and y, we have three equations (i), (ii) and (iii).
From equation (i),
x + 3 = 5
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 5 – 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = 2
From equation (ii),
y – 4 = 3
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 3 + 4
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} y = 7
We need not solve equation (iii) as we have got the values of x and y.
Thus, the values of x = 2 and y = 7.

question_html: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&1 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} show that A2 - 5A + 7I = 0.
question_text: If {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&1 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} show that A 2 - 5A + 7I = 0.
explanation: Given: {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&1 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} A 2 – 5A + 7I {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&1 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&1 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right] - 5\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&1 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right] + 7\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {9 - 1}&{3 + 2} \\ { - 3 - 2}&{ - 1 + 4} \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {15}&5 \\ { - 5}&{10} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&0 \\ 0&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8&5 \\ { - 5}&3 \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {15}&5 \\ { - 5}&{10} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&0 \\ 0&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {8 - 15}&{5 - 5} \\ { - 5 + 5}&{3 - 10} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&0 \\ 0&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 7}&0 \\ 0&{ - 7} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&0 \\ 0&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 7 + 7}&{0 + 7} \\ {0 + 7}&{ - 7 + 7} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0 \\ 0&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} L.H.S= R.H.S. Hence Proved.
solution: Given: {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&1 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} A 2 – 5A + 7I {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&1 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&1 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right] - 5\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&1 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right] + 7\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {9 - 1}&{3 + 2} \\ { - 3 - 2}&{ - 1 + 4} \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {15}&5 \\ { - 5}&{10} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&0 \\ 0&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8&5 \\ { - 5}&3 \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {15}&5 \\ { - 5}&{10} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&0 \\ 0&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {8 - 15}&{5 - 5} \\ { - 5 + 5}&{3 - 10} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&0 \\ 0&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 7}&0 \\ 0&{ - 7} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&0 \\ 0&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 7 + 7}&{0 + 7} \\ {0 + 7}&{ - 7 + 7} \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0 \\ 0&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore {/tex} L.H.S= R.H.S. Hence Proved.
solution_html:
Given: {tex}A = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&1 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} A2 – 5A + 7I {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&1 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right]\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&1 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right] - 5\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 3&1 \\ { - 1}&2 \end{array}} \right] + 7\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&0 \\ 0&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {9 - 1}&{3 + 2} \\ { - 3 - 2}&{ - 1 + 4} \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {15}&5 \\ { - 5}&{10} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&0 \\ 0&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 8&5 \\ { - 5}&3 \end{array}} \right] - \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {15}&5 \\ { - 5}&{10} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&0 \\ 0&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} {8 - 15}&{5 - 5} \\ { - 5 + 5}&{3 - 10} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&0 \\ 0&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 7}&0 \\ 0&{ - 7} \end{array}} \right] + \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 7&0 \\ 0&7 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} { - 7 + 7}&{0 + 7} \\ {0 + 7}&{ - 7 + 7} \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex} = \left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 0&0 \\ 0&0 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore {/tex} L.H.S= R.H.S. Hence Proved.

question_html: Find the value of x such that {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&x&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex}{tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr}{1} & {3} & {2} \\ {2} & {5} & {1} \\ {15} & {3} & {2}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l}{1} \\ {2} \\ {x}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = 0.
question_text: Find the value of x such that {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&x&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr}{1} & {3} & {2} \\ {2} & {5} & {1} \\ {15} & {3} & {2}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l}{1} \\ {2} \\ {x}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = 0.
explanation: We have, {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&x&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc}{1} & {3} & {2} \\ {2} & {5} & {1} \\ {15} & {3} & {2}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l}{1} \\ {2} \\ {x}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&x&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c}{7+2 x} \\ {12+x} \\ {21+2 x}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 7 + 2x + 12x + x 2 + 21 + 2x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 + 16x + 28 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x + 14)(x + 2) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -2 or -14.
solution: We have, {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&x&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc}{1} & {3} & {2} \\ {2} & {5} & {1} \\ {15} & {3} & {2}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l}{1} \\ {2} \\ {x}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&x&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c}{7+2 x} \\ {12+x} \\ {21+2 x}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 7 + 2x + 12x + x 2 + 21 + 2x = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x 2 + 16x + 28 = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x + 14)(x + 2) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -2 or -14.
solution_html:
We have,
{tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&x&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ccc}{1} & {3} & {2} \\ {2} & {5} & {1} \\ {15} & {3} & {2}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{l}{1} \\ {2} \\ {x}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left[ {\begin{array}{*{20}{c}} 1&x&1 \end{array}} \right]{/tex} {tex}\left[\begin{array}{c}{7+2 x} \\ {12+x} \\ {21+2 x}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 7 + 2x + 12x + x2 + 21 + 2x = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x2 + 16x + 28 = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} (x + 14)(x + 2) = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} x = -2 or -14.

question_html: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{1} & {0} \\ {-1} & {7}\end{array}\right]{/tex} and I = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex}, then find k so that A2 = 8A + kI.
question_text: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{1} & {0} \\ {-1} & {7}\end{array}\right]{/tex} and I = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} , then find k so that A 2 = 8A + kI.
explanation: We have, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{1} & {0} \\ {-1} & {7}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} A 2 = AA = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{1} & {0} \\ {-1} & {7}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr}{1} & {0} \\ {-1} & {7}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{1} & {0} \\ {-8} & {49}\end{array}\right]{/tex} and 8A + kI = 8 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{1} & {0} \\ {-1} & {7}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + k {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{8} & {0} \\ {-8} & {56}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{k} & {0} \\ {0} & {k}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{8+k} & {0} \\ {-8} & {56+k}\end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, A 2 = 8A + kI {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{1} & {0} \\ {-8} & {49}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{8+k} & {0} \\ {-8} & {56+k}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = 8 + k and 56 + k = 49 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = -7
solution: We have, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{1} & {0} \\ {-1} & {7}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\therefore{/tex} A 2 = AA = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{1} & {0} \\ {-1} & {7}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr}{1} & {0} \\ {-1} & {7}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{1} & {0} \\ {-8} & {49}\end{array}\right]{/tex} and 8A + kI = 8 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{1} & {0} \\ {-1} & {7}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + k {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{8} & {0} \\ {-8} & {56}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{k} & {0} \\ {0} & {k}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{8+k} & {0} \\ {-8} & {56+k}\end{array}\right]{/tex} Now, A 2 = 8A + kI {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{1} & {0} \\ {-8} & {49}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{8+k} & {0} \\ {-8} & {56+k}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = 8 + k and 56 + k = 49 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = -7
solution_html:
We have, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{1} & {0} \\ {-1} & {7}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\therefore{/tex} A2 = AA = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{1} & {0} \\ {-1} & {7}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr}{1} & {0} \\ {-1} & {7}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{1} & {0} \\ {-8} & {49}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
and 8A + kI = 8{tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{1} & {0} \\ {-1} & {7}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + k{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{8} & {0} \\ {-8} & {56}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{k} & {0} \\ {0} & {k}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{8+k} & {0} \\ {-8} & {56+k}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now, A2 = 8A + kI
{tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{1} & {0} \\ {-8} & {49}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{8+k} & {0} \\ {-8} & {56+k}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} 1 = 8 + k and 56 + k = 49
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} k = -7

question_html: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{2} & {-1} \\ {3} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{0} & {4} \\ {-1} & {7}\end{array}\right]{/tex}, find 3A2 - 2B + I.
question_text: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{2} & {-1} \\ {3} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{0} & {4} \\ {-1} & {7}\end{array}\right]{/tex} , find 3A 2 - 2B + I.
explanation: Given, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2} & {-1} \\ {3} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{0} & {4} \\ {-1} & {7}\end{array}\right]{/tex} 3A 2 - 2B + I = 3 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2} & {-1} \\ {3} & {2}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2} & {-1} \\ {3} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - 2 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{0} & {4} \\ {-1} & {7}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = 3 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{4-3} & {-2-2} \\ {6+6} & {-3+4}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{0} & {8} \\ {-2} & {14}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = 3 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{1} & {-4} \\ {12} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{0} & {8} \\ {-2} & {14}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{3} & {-12} \\ {36} & {3}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{0} & {8} \\ {-2} & {14}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{3-0+1} & {-12-8+0} \\ {36+2+0} & {3-14+1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{4} & {-20} \\ {38} & {-10}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution: Given, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2} & {-1} \\ {3} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{0} & {4} \\ {-1} & {7}\end{array}\right]{/tex} 3A 2 - 2B + I = 3 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2} & {-1} \\ {3} & {2}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2} & {-1} \\ {3} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - 2 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{0} & {4} \\ {-1} & {7}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = 3 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{4-3} & {-2-2} \\ {6+6} & {-3+4}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{0} & {8} \\ {-2} & {14}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = 3 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{1} & {-4} \\ {12} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{0} & {8} \\ {-2} & {14}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{3} & {-12} \\ {36} & {3}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{0} & {8} \\ {-2} & {14}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{3-0+1} & {-12-8+0} \\ {36+2+0} & {3-14+1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{4} & {-20} \\ {38} & {-10}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2} & {-1} \\ {3} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} and B = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{0} & {4} \\ {-1} & {7}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
3A2 - 2B + I
= 3{tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2} & {-1} \\ {3} & {2}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2} & {-1} \\ {3} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - 2 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{0} & {4} \\ {-1} & {7}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
= 3{tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{4-3} & {-2-2} \\ {6+6} & {-3+4}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{0} & {8} \\ {-2} & {14}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
= 3 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{1} & {-4} \\ {12} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{0} & {8} \\ {-2} & {14}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{3} & {-12} \\ {36} & {3}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{0} & {8} \\ {-2} & {14}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{3-0+1} & {-12-8+0} \\ {36+2+0} & {3-14+1}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{4} & {-20} \\ {38} & {-10}\end{array}\right]{/tex}

question_html: Show that the matrix A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{2} & {3} \\ {1} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} satisfies the equation A3 - 4A2 + A = O.
question_text: Show that the matrix A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{2} & {3} \\ {1} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} satisfies the equation A 3 - 4A 2 + A = O.
explanation: Given, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{2} & {3} \\ {1} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} A 2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{2} & {3} \\ {1} & {2}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll}{2} & {3} \\ {1} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{4+3} & {6+6} \\ {2+2} & {3+4}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{7} & {12} \\ {4} & {7}\end{array}\right]{/tex} A 3 = A 2 {tex}\cdot{/tex} A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{7} & {12} \\ {4} & {7}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2} & {3} \\ {1} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{14+12} & {21+24} \\ {8+7} & {12+14}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{26} & {45} \\ {15} & {26}\end{array}\right]{/tex} Hence, A 3 - 4A 2 + A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{26} & {45} \\ {15} & {26}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - 4 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{7} & {12} \\ {4} & {7}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{2} & {3} \\ {1} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{26-28+2} & {45-48+3} \\ {15-16+1} & {26-28+2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = 0 So, A 3 - 4A 2 + A = 0
solution: Given, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{2} & {3} \\ {1} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} A 2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{2} & {3} \\ {1} & {2}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll}{2} & {3} \\ {1} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{4+3} & {6+6} \\ {2+2} & {3+4}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{7} & {12} \\ {4} & {7}\end{array}\right]{/tex} A 3 = A 2 {tex}\cdot{/tex} A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{7} & {12} \\ {4} & {7}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2} & {3} \\ {1} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{14+12} & {21+24} \\ {8+7} & {12+14}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{26} & {45} \\ {15} & {26}\end{array}\right]{/tex} Hence, A 3 - 4A 2 + A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{26} & {45} \\ {15} & {26}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - 4 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{7} & {12} \\ {4} & {7}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{2} & {3} \\ {1} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{26-28+2} & {45-48+3} \\ {15-16+1} & {26-28+2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = 0 So, A 3 - 4A 2 + A = 0
solution_html:
Given, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{2} & {3} \\ {1} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
A2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{2} & {3} \\ {1} & {2}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll}{2} & {3} \\ {1} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{4+3} & {6+6} \\ {2+2} & {3+4}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{7} & {12} \\ {4} & {7}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
A3 = A2{tex}\cdot{/tex}A
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{7} & {12} \\ {4} & {7}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc}{2} & {3} \\ {1} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{14+12} & {21+24} \\ {8+7} & {12+14}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{26} & {45} \\ {15} & {26}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
Hence, A3 - 4A2 + A
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{26} & {45} \\ {15} & {26}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - 4{tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{7} & {12} \\ {4} & {7}\end{array}\right]{/tex} + {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{2} & {3} \\ {1} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{26-28+2} & {45-48+3} \\ {15-16+1} & {26-28+2}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
= 0
So, A3 - 4A2 + A = 0

question_html: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {2} \\ {2} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} and f(x) = x2 - 2x - 3, show that f(A) = O.
question_text: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {2} \\ {2} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} and f(x) = x 2 - 2x - 3, show that f(A) = O.
explanation: Given, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {2} \\ {2} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} and f(x) = x 2 - 2x - 3 f(A) = A 2 - 2A - 3I = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {2} \\ {2} & {1}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {2} \\ {2} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - 2 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {2} \\ {2} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - 3 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1+4} & {2+2} \\ {2+2} & {4+1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{2} & {4} \\ {4} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{3} & {0} \\ {0} & {3}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{5} & {4} \\ {4} & {5}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{2} & {4} \\ {4} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{3} & {0} \\ {0} & {3}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{5-2-3} & {4-4-0} \\ {4-4-0} & {5-2-3}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = 0 So, f(A) = 0
solution: Given, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {2} \\ {2} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} and f(x) = x 2 - 2x - 3 f(A) = A 2 - 2A - 3I = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {2} \\ {2} & {1}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {2} \\ {2} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - 2 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {2} \\ {2} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - 3 {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1+4} & {2+2} \\ {2+2} & {4+1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{2} & {4} \\ {4} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{3} & {0} \\ {0} & {3}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{5} & {4} \\ {4} & {5}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{2} & {4} \\ {4} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{3} & {0} \\ {0} & {3}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{5-2-3} & {4-4-0} \\ {4-4-0} & {5-2-3}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = 0 So, f(A) = 0
solution_html:
Given,
A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {2} \\ {2} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} and f(x) = x2 - 2x - 3
f(A) = A2 - 2A - 3I = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {2} \\ {2} & {1}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {2} \\ {2} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - 2{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {2} \\ {2} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - 3{tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1} & {0} \\ {0} & {1}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{1+4} & {2+2} \\ {2+2} & {4+1}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{2} & {4} \\ {4} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{3} & {0} \\ {0} & {3}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{5} & {4} \\ {4} & {5}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{2} & {4} \\ {4} & {2}\end{array}\right]{/tex} - {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{3} & {0} \\ {0} & {3}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{5-2-3} & {4-4-0} \\ {4-4-0} & {5-2-3}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{ll}{0} & {0} \\ {0} & {0}\end{array}\right]{/tex}= 0
So, f(A) = 0

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}A_{\alpha}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{\cos \alpha} & {\sin \alpha} \\ {-\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha}\end{array}\right]{/tex}, then prove that {tex}\left(A_{\alpha}\right)^{n}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos n \alpha} & {\sin n \alpha} \\ {-\sin n \alpha} & {\cos n \alpha}\end{array}\right]{/tex} for every positive integer n.
question_text: If {tex}A_{\alpha}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{\cos \alpha} & {\sin \alpha} \\ {-\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha}\end{array}\right]{/tex} , then prove that {tex}\left(A_{\alpha}\right)^{n}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos n \alpha} & {\sin n \alpha} \\ {-\sin n \alpha} & {\cos n \alpha}\end{array}\right]{/tex} for every positive integer n.
explanation: We shall prove the result by mathematical induction on n. Step 1: When n = 1, by the definition of integral powers of a matrix, we have {tex}\left(A_{\alpha}\right)^{1}{/tex} = {tex}A_{\alpha}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr}{\cos \alpha} & {\sin \alpha} \\ {-\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos (1. \alpha)} & {\sin (1. \alpha)} \\ {-\sin (1. \alpha)} & {\cos (1. \alpha)}\end{array}\right]{/tex} So, the result is true for n = 1. Step 2: Let the result be true for n = m. Then, {tex}\left(A_{\alpha}\right)^{m}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{\cos m \alpha} & {\sin m \alpha} \\ {-\sin m \alpha} & {\cos m \alpha}\end{array}\right]{/tex} ......(i) Now we will show that the result is true for n = m + 1. i.e. {tex}\left(A_{\alpha}\right)^{m+1}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{\cos (m+1) \alpha} & {\sin (m+1) \alpha} \\ {-\sin (m+1) \alpha} & {\cos (m+1) \alpha}\end{array}\right]{/tex} By definition of integral powers of a square matrix, we have {tex}\left(A_{\alpha}\right)^{m+1}{/tex} = {tex}\left(A_{\alpha}\right)^{m} A_{\alpha}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(A_{\alpha}\right)^{m+1}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr}{\cos m \alpha} & {\sin m \alpha} \\ {-\sin m \alpha} & {\cos m\alpha}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr}{\cos \alpha} & {\sin \alpha} \\ {-\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha}\end{array}\right]{/tex} [By assum[tion (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(A_{\alpha}\right)^{m+1}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos m \alpha \cos \alpha-\sin m \alpha \sin \alpha} & {\cos m \alpha \sin \alpha+\sin m \alpha \cos \alpha} \\ {-\sin m \alpha \cos \alpha-\cos m \alpha \sin \alpha} & {-\sin m \alpha \sin \alpha+\cos m \alpha \cos \alpha}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(A_{\alpha}\right)^{m+1}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{\cos (m \alpha+\alpha)} & {\sin (m \alpha+\alpha)} \\ {-\sin (m \alpha+\alpha)} & {\cos (m \alpha+\alpha)}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{\cos (m+1) \alpha} & {\sin (m+1) \alpha} \\ {-\sin (m+1) \alpha} & {\cos (m+1) \alpha}\end{array}\right]{/tex} This shows that the result is true for n = m + 1, wherever it is true for n = m. Hence, by the principle of mathematical induction, the result is valid for any positive integer n.
solution: We shall prove the result by mathematical induction on n. Step 1: When n = 1, by the definition of integral powers of a matrix, we have {tex}\left(A_{\alpha}\right)^{1}{/tex} = {tex}A_{\alpha}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr}{\cos \alpha} & {\sin \alpha} \\ {-\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos (1. \alpha)} & {\sin (1. \alpha)} \\ {-\sin (1. \alpha)} & {\cos (1. \alpha)}\end{array}\right]{/tex} So, the result is true for n = 1. Step 2: Let the result be true for n = m. Then, {tex}\left(A_{\alpha}\right)^{m}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{\cos m \alpha} & {\sin m \alpha} \\ {-\sin m \alpha} & {\cos m \alpha}\end{array}\right]{/tex} ......(i) Now we will show that the result is true for n = m + 1. i.e. {tex}\left(A_{\alpha}\right)^{m+1}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{\cos (m+1) \alpha} & {\sin (m+1) \alpha} \\ {-\sin (m+1) \alpha} & {\cos (m+1) \alpha}\end{array}\right]{/tex} By definition of integral powers of a square matrix, we have {tex}\left(A_{\alpha}\right)^{m+1}{/tex} = {tex}\left(A_{\alpha}\right)^{m} A_{\alpha}{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(A_{\alpha}\right)^{m+1}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr}{\cos m \alpha} & {\sin m \alpha} \\ {-\sin m \alpha} & {\cos m\alpha}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr}{\cos \alpha} & {\sin \alpha} \\ {-\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha}\end{array}\right]{/tex} [By assum[tion (i)] {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(A_{\alpha}\right)^{m+1}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos m \alpha \cos \alpha-\sin m \alpha \sin \alpha} & {\cos m \alpha \sin \alpha+\sin m \alpha \cos \alpha} \\ {-\sin m \alpha \cos \alpha-\cos m \alpha \sin \alpha} & {-\sin m \alpha \sin \alpha+\cos m \alpha \cos \alpha}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(A_{\alpha}\right)^{m+1}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{\cos (m \alpha+\alpha)} & {\sin (m \alpha+\alpha)} \\ {-\sin (m \alpha+\alpha)} & {\cos (m \alpha+\alpha)}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{\cos (m+1) \alpha} & {\sin (m+1) \alpha} \\ {-\sin (m+1) \alpha} & {\cos (m+1) \alpha}\end{array}\right]{/tex} This shows that the result is true for n = m + 1, wherever it is true for n = m. Hence, by the principle of mathematical induction, the result is valid for any positive integer n.
solution_html:
We shall prove the result by mathematical induction on n.
Step 1: When n = 1, by the definition of integral powers of a matrix, we have
{tex}\left(A_{\alpha}\right)^{1}{/tex} = {tex}A_{\alpha}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr}{\cos \alpha} & {\sin \alpha} \\ {-\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos (1. \alpha)} & {\sin (1. \alpha)} \\ {-\sin (1. \alpha)} & {\cos (1. \alpha)}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
So, the result is true for n = 1.
Step 2: Let the result be true for n = m. Then,
{tex}\left(A_{\alpha}\right)^{m}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{\cos m \alpha} & {\sin m \alpha} \\ {-\sin m \alpha} & {\cos m \alpha}\end{array}\right]{/tex}  ......(i)
Now we will show that the result is true for n = m + 1.
i.e. {tex}\left(A_{\alpha}\right)^{m+1}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{\cos (m+1) \alpha} & {\sin (m+1) \alpha} \\ {-\sin (m+1) \alpha} & {\cos (m+1) \alpha}\end{array}\right]{/tex} 
By definition of integral powers of a square matrix, we have
{tex}\left(A_{\alpha}\right)^{m+1}{/tex} = {tex}\left(A_{\alpha}\right)^{m} A_{\alpha}{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(A_{\alpha}\right)^{m+1}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr}{\cos m \alpha} & {\sin m \alpha} \\ {-\sin m \alpha} & {\cos m\alpha}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr}{\cos \alpha} & {\sin \alpha} \\ {-\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha}\end{array}\right]{/tex} [By assum[tion (i)]
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(A_{\alpha}\right)^{m+1}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos m \alpha \cos \alpha-\sin m \alpha \sin \alpha} & {\cos m \alpha \sin \alpha+\sin m \alpha \cos \alpha} \\ {-\sin m \alpha \cos \alpha-\cos m \alpha \sin \alpha} & {-\sin m \alpha \sin \alpha+\cos m \alpha \cos \alpha}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}\left(A_{\alpha}\right)^{m+1}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{\cos (m \alpha+\alpha)} & {\sin (m \alpha+\alpha)} \\ {-\sin (m \alpha+\alpha)} & {\cos (m \alpha+\alpha)}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{\cos (m+1) \alpha} & {\sin (m+1) \alpha} \\ {-\sin (m+1) \alpha} & {\cos (m+1) \alpha}\end{array}\right]{/tex} 
This shows that the result is true for n = m + 1, wherever it is true for n = m.
Hence, by the principle of mathematical induction, the result is valid for any positive integer n.

subject: Mathematics
question_html: If {tex}A_{\alpha}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rcc}{\cos \alpha} & {\sin \alpha} \\ {-\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha}\end{array}\right]{/tex}, then prove that {tex}A_{\alpha} A_{\beta}=A_{\alpha+\beta}{/tex}
question_text: If {tex}A_{\alpha}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rcc}{\cos \alpha} & {\sin \alpha} \\ {-\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha}\end{array}\right]{/tex} , then prove that {tex}A_{\alpha} A_{\beta}=A_{\alpha+\beta}{/tex}
explanation: We have, {tex}A_{\alpha} A_{\beta}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{\cos \alpha} & {\sin \alpha} \\ {-\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr}{\cos \beta} & {\sin \beta} \\ {-\sin \beta} & {\cos \beta}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}A_{\alpha} A_{\beta}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos \alpha \cos \beta-\sin \alpha \sin \beta} & {\sin \alpha \cos \beta+\cos \alpha \sin \beta} \\ {-\sin \alpha \cos \beta-\cos \alpha \sin \beta} & {\cos \alpha \cos \beta-\sin \alpha \sin \beta}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}A_{\alpha} A_{\beta}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{\cos (\alpha+\beta)} & {\sin (\alpha+\beta)} \\ {-\sin (\alpha+\beta)} & {\cos (\alpha+\beta)}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}A_{\alpha+\beta}{/tex}
solution: We have, {tex}A_{\alpha} A_{\beta}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{\cos \alpha} & {\sin \alpha} \\ {-\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr}{\cos \beta} & {\sin \beta} \\ {-\sin \beta} & {\cos \beta}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}A_{\alpha} A_{\beta}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos \alpha \cos \beta-\sin \alpha \sin \beta} & {\sin \alpha \cos \beta+\cos \alpha \sin \beta} \\ {-\sin \alpha \cos \beta-\cos \alpha \sin \beta} & {\cos \alpha \cos \beta-\sin \alpha \sin \beta}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}A_{\alpha} A_{\beta}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{\cos (\alpha+\beta)} & {\sin (\alpha+\beta)} \\ {-\sin (\alpha+\beta)} & {\cos (\alpha+\beta)}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}A_{\alpha+\beta}{/tex}
solution_html:
We have,
{tex}A_{\alpha} A_{\beta}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{\cos \alpha} & {\sin \alpha} \\ {-\sin \alpha} & {\cos \alpha}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{rr}{\cos \beta} & {\sin \beta} \\ {-\sin \beta} & {\cos \beta}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}A_{\alpha} A_{\beta}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos \alpha \cos \beta-\sin \alpha \sin \beta} & {\sin \alpha \cos \beta+\cos \alpha \sin \beta} \\ {-\sin \alpha \cos \beta-\cos \alpha \sin \beta} & {\cos \alpha \cos \beta-\sin \alpha \sin \beta}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} {tex}A_{\alpha} A_{\beta}{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rr}{\cos (\alpha+\beta)} & {\sin (\alpha+\beta)} \\ {-\sin (\alpha+\beta)} & {\cos (\alpha+\beta)}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}A_{\alpha+\beta}{/tex}

question_html: Construct a 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix A = [aij], whose element aij = {tex}\frac{(i+2j)^{2}}{2}{/tex}.
question_text: Construct a 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix A = [a ij ], whose element a ij = {tex}\frac{(i+2j)^{2}}{2}{/tex} .
explanation: Since it is a 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix it has 2 rows and 2 column. Let matrix be A Where {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now it is given that {tex}\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{ij}}=\frac{(\mathrm{i}+2 \mathrm{j})^2}{2}{/tex} {tex}a_{11}=\frac{(1+2(1))^2}{2}=\frac{(1+2)^2}{2}=\frac{(3)^2}{2}=\frac{9}{2}{/tex} {tex}a_{12}=\frac{(1+2(2))^2}{2}=\frac{(1+4)^2}{2}=\frac{(5)^2}{2}=\frac{25}{2}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{a}_{21}=\frac{(2+2(1))^2}{2}=\frac{(2+2)^2}{2}=\frac{(4)^2}{2}=\frac{16}{2}{/tex} = 8 {tex}\mathrm{a}_{22}=\frac{(2+2(2))^2}{2}=\frac{(2+4)^2}{2}=\frac{(6)^2}{2}=\frac{36}{2}{/tex} = 18 Hence, the required matrix A is {tex}A\left[\begin{array}{ll} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \frac{9}{2} & \frac{25}{2} \\ 8 & 18 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution: Since it is a 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix it has 2 rows and 2 column. Let matrix be A Where {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array}\right]{/tex} Now it is given that {tex}\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{ij}}=\frac{(\mathrm{i}+2 \mathrm{j})^2}{2}{/tex} {tex}a_{11}=\frac{(1+2(1))^2}{2}=\frac{(1+2)^2}{2}=\frac{(3)^2}{2}=\frac{9}{2}{/tex} {tex}a_{12}=\frac{(1+2(2))^2}{2}=\frac{(1+4)^2}{2}=\frac{(5)^2}{2}=\frac{25}{2}{/tex} {tex}\mathrm{a}_{21}=\frac{(2+2(1))^2}{2}=\frac{(2+2)^2}{2}=\frac{(4)^2}{2}=\frac{16}{2}{/tex} = 8 {tex}\mathrm{a}_{22}=\frac{(2+2(2))^2}{2}=\frac{(2+4)^2}{2}=\frac{(6)^2}{2}=\frac{36}{2}{/tex} = 18 Hence, the required matrix A is {tex}A\left[\begin{array}{ll} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \frac{9}{2} & \frac{25}{2} \\ 8 & 18 \end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Since it is a 2 {tex}\times{/tex} 2 matrix
it has 2 rows and 2 column.
Let matrix be A
Where {tex}A=\left[\begin{array}{ll} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array}\right]{/tex}
Now it is given that
{tex}\mathrm{a}_{\mathrm{ij}}=\frac{(\mathrm{i}+2 \mathrm{j})^2}{2}{/tex}
{tex}a_{11}=\frac{(1+2(1))^2}{2}=\frac{(1+2)^2}{2}=\frac{(3)^2}{2}=\frac{9}{2}{/tex}
{tex}a_{12}=\frac{(1+2(2))^2}{2}=\frac{(1+4)^2}{2}=\frac{(5)^2}{2}=\frac{25}{2}{/tex}
{tex}\mathrm{a}_{21}=\frac{(2+2(1))^2}{2}=\frac{(2+2)^2}{2}=\frac{(4)^2}{2}=\frac{16}{2}{/tex} = 8
{tex}\mathrm{a}_{22}=\frac{(2+2(2))^2}{2}=\frac{(2+4)^2}{2}=\frac{(6)^2}{2}=\frac{36}{2}{/tex} = 18
Hence,
the required matrix A is
{tex}A\left[\begin{array}{ll} a_{11} & a_{12} \\ a_{21} & a_{22} \end{array}\right]=\left[\begin{array}{cc} \frac{9}{2} & \frac{25}{2} \\ 8 & 18 \end{array}\right]{/tex}

question_html: Find a matrix X such that 2A + B + X = 0, where A = {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { r r } { - 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 3 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} and, B = {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 3 } & { - 2 } \\ { 1 } & { 5 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
question_text: Find a matrix X such that 2A + B + X = 0, where A = {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { r r } { - 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 3 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} and, B = {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 3 } & { - 2 } \\ { 1 } & { 5 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
explanation: We have, 2A + B + X = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} X = - 2A - B {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} X = - 2 {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { r r } { - 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 3 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right] - \left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 3 } & { - 2 } \\ { 1 } & { 5 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} X = {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 2 } & { - 4 } \\ { - 6 } & { - 8 } \end{array} \right] + \left[ \begin{array} { r r } { - 3 } & { 2 } \\ { - 1 } & { - 5 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 2 - 3 } & { - 4 + 2 } \\ { - 6 - 1 } & { - 8 - 5 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { - 1 } & { - 2 } \\ { - 7 } & { - 13 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
solution: We have, 2A + B + X = 0 {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} X = - 2A - B {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} X = - 2 {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { r r } { - 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 3 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right] - \left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 3 } & { - 2 } \\ { 1 } & { 5 } \end{array} \right]{/tex} {tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} X = {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 2 } & { - 4 } \\ { - 6 } & { - 8 } \end{array} \right] + \left[ \begin{array} { r r } { - 3 } & { 2 } \\ { - 1 } & { - 5 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 2 - 3 } & { - 4 + 2 } \\ { - 6 - 1 } & { - 8 - 5 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { - 1 } & { - 2 } \\ { - 7 } & { - 13 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
solution_html:
We have,
2A + B + X = 0
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} X = - 2A - B
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} X = - 2 {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { r r } { - 1 } & { 2 } \\ { 3 } & { 4 } \end{array} \right] - \left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 3 } & { - 2 } \\ { 1 } & { 5 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}
{tex}\Rightarrow{/tex} X = {tex}\left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 2 } & { - 4 } \\ { - 6 } & { - 8 } \end{array} \right] + \left[ \begin{array} { r r } { - 3 } & { 2 } \\ { - 1 } & { - 5 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { r r } { 2 - 3 } & { - 4 + 2 } \\ { - 6 - 1 } & { - 8 - 5 } \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array} { c c } { - 1 } & { - 2 } \\ { - 7 } & { - 13 } \end{array} \right]{/tex}

question_text: If A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{rrr}{\cos 2 \theta} & {\sin 2 \theta} \\ {-\sin 2 \theta} & {\cos 2 \theta}\end{array}\right]{/tex} , find A 2 .
explanation: Given, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos 2 \theta} & {\sin 2 \theta} \\ {-\sin 2 \theta} & {\cos 2 \theta}\end{array}\right]{/tex} A 2 = A {tex}\cdot{/tex} A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos 2 \theta} & {\sin 2 \theta} \\ {-\sin 2 \theta} & {\cos 2 \theta}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos 2 \theta} & {\sin 2 \theta} \\ {-\sin 2 \theta} & {\cos 2 \theta}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos ^{2} 2 \theta-\sin ^{2} 2 \theta} & {\cos 2 \theta \sin {2\theta}+\cos 2 \theta \sin {2} \theta} \\ {-\cos 2 \theta \sin {2} \theta-\sin {2} \theta \cos {2} \theta} & {-\sin ^{2} 2 \theta+\cos ^{2} 2 \theta}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos 4 \theta} & {2 \sin {2} \theta \cos {2} \theta} \\ {-2 \sin {2\theta} \cos 2 \theta} & {\cos 4 \theta}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {since cos 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} - sin 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} = cos 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} } = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos 4 \theta} & {\sin 4 \theta} \\ {-\sin 4 \theta} & {\cos 4 \theta}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {since sin2 {tex}\theta{/tex} = 2 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} } Hence, A 2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos 4 \theta} & {\sin 4 \theta} \\ {-\sin 4 \theta} & {\cos 4 \theta}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution: Given, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos 2 \theta} & {\sin 2 \theta} \\ {-\sin 2 \theta} & {\cos 2 \theta}\end{array}\right]{/tex} A 2 = A {tex}\cdot{/tex} A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos 2 \theta} & {\sin 2 \theta} \\ {-\sin 2 \theta} & {\cos 2 \theta}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos 2 \theta} & {\sin 2 \theta} \\ {-\sin 2 \theta} & {\cos 2 \theta}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos ^{2} 2 \theta-\sin ^{2} 2 \theta} & {\cos 2 \theta \sin {2\theta}+\cos 2 \theta \sin {2} \theta} \\ {-\cos 2 \theta \sin {2} \theta-\sin {2} \theta \cos {2} \theta} & {-\sin ^{2} 2 \theta+\cos ^{2} 2 \theta}\end{array}\right]{/tex} = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos 4 \theta} & {2 \sin {2} \theta \cos {2} \theta} \\ {-2 \sin {2\theta} \cos 2 \theta} & {\cos 4 \theta}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {since cos 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} - sin 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} = cos 2 {tex}\theta{/tex} } = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos 4 \theta} & {\sin 4 \theta} \\ {-\sin 4 \theta} & {\cos 4 \theta}\end{array}\right]{/tex} {since sin2 {tex}\theta{/tex} = 2 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex} } Hence, A 2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos 4 \theta} & {\sin 4 \theta} \\ {-\sin 4 \theta} & {\cos 4 \theta}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
solution_html:
Given, A = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos 2 \theta} & {\sin 2 \theta} \\ {-\sin 2 \theta} & {\cos 2 \theta}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
A2 = A{tex}\cdot{/tex}A
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos 2 \theta} & {\sin 2 \theta} \\ {-\sin 2 \theta} & {\cos 2 \theta}\end{array}\right]\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos 2 \theta} & {\sin 2 \theta} \\ {-\sin 2 \theta} & {\cos 2 \theta}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos ^{2} 2 \theta-\sin ^{2} 2 \theta} & {\cos 2 \theta \sin {2\theta}+\cos 2 \theta \sin {2} \theta} \\ {-\cos 2 \theta \sin {2} \theta-\sin {2} \theta \cos {2} \theta} & {-\sin ^{2} 2 \theta+\cos ^{2} 2 \theta}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos 4 \theta} & {2 \sin {2} \theta \cos {2} \theta} \\ {-2 \sin {2\theta} \cos 2 \theta} & {\cos 4 \theta}\end{array}\right]{/tex}  {since cos2 {tex}\theta{/tex} - sin2 {tex}\theta{/tex} = cos 2{tex}\theta{/tex} }
= {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos 4 \theta} & {\sin 4 \theta} \\ {-\sin 4 \theta} & {\cos 4 \theta}\end{array}\right]{/tex}  {since sin2{tex}\theta{/tex} = 2 sin {tex}\theta{/tex} cos {tex}\theta{/tex}}
Hence,
A2 = {tex}\left[\begin{array}{cc}{\cos 4 \theta} & {\sin 4 \theta} \\ {-\sin 4 \theta} & {\cos 4 \theta}\end{array}\right]{/tex}
